top_papers.title: WildClawBench: A Benchmark for Real-World, Long-Horizon Agent Evaluation
top_papers.abstract: WildClawBench introduces a novel benchmark designed to evaluate real-world, long-horizon agent performance by executing tasks within a native runtime environment using actual command-line tools inside reproducible Docker containers. Its core contribution is providing a challenging, realistic evaluation suite of 60 multimodal tasks, averaging eight minutes and many tool calls, to bridge the gap between synthetic benchmarks and practical agent deployment. Evaluation employs a hybrid grading system combining rule-based checks, environment auditing, and LLM/VLM judges for comprehensive assessment.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison with previous agent benchmarks and WildClawBench. (a) Prior benchmarks evaluate short-horizon, single-step tasks with toy APIs in controlled sandboxes, whereas (b) WildClawBench evaluates long-horizon multimodal workflows with real tools in open-world environments. (c) The benchmark spans six categories and is compatible with multiple agent harnesses. (d) A summary of key differences across environment, task horizon, tool use, and evaluation.

top_papers.title: ClinSeekAgent: Automating Multimodal Evidence Seeking for Agentic Clinical Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: ClinSeekAgent is an automated framework that empowers LLMs to actively seek and synthesize multimodal clinical evidence from diverse sources like knowledge bases, EHRs, and imaging tools. Its core method involves iterative planning and hypothesis refinement, moving beyond passive evidence consumption to dynamic evidence acquisition. The contribution is a paradigm shift towards agentic clinical reasoning that can operate with raw data, enabling more grounded and comprehensive decision support.
top_papers.figure_caption: ClinSeekAgent Overview. ClinSeekAgent is an automated agentic evidence-seeking pipeline. It interacts with heterogeneous data sources to enable multimodal evidence seeking for clinical decision support. Compared with prior user-curated context settings, ClinSeekAgent is more flexible by acquiring richer information and knowledge from diverse tools.

top_papers.title: KoRe: Compact Knowledge Representations for Large Language Models
top_papers.abstract: KoRe introduces a method to encode 1-hop knowledge graph subgraphs into compact, discrete tokens. These tokens are then injected into a Large Language Model (LLM) backbone, allowing for efficient integration of external knowledge without extensive retraining. This approach aims to overcome the opacity and update difficulties of LLM-internal knowledge by leveraging the readability and editability of knowledge graphs.
top_papers.figure_caption: Taxonomy of Knowledge Augmentation Approaches for LLMs

top_papers.title: TIDE: Efficient and Lossless MoE Diffusion LLM Inference with I/O-aware Expert Offload
top_papers.abstract: TIDE addresses the challenge of efficiently inferring large Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) diffusion LLMs on resource-constrained devices. Its core method is an I/O-aware expert offload strategy that exploits the temporal stability of expert activations during the diffusion process. This allows for efficient expert placement and refresh, minimizing I/O overhead and compute bottlenecks, thus enabling lossless and efficient MoE dLLM inference.
top_papers.figure_caption: (a) Similarity heatmap of expert routing across denoising steps within a block. Expert routing remains highly similar for nearby steps, and the diagonal bands show that this stability extends beyond immediate neighbors: step pairs separated by five denoising steps retain cosine similarity near 0.95 0.95 . (b) Overview of TIDE . At refresh steps , the system intelligently swaps the GPU and CPU experts based on token hit counts (number of tokens each expert has processed). At skipped steps , the system continues decoding with the current expert placement and does not migrate experts. By exploiting routing stability across adjacent steps, TIDE avoids unnecessary GPU-CPU I/O overhead and maintains high GPU utilization. (c) Throughput comparison of TIDE against state-of-the-art MoE inference solutions [Kamahori et al. , 2024 , Eliseev and Mazur, 2023 ] for LLaDA2.0 in a single GPU-CPU setting.

top_papers.title: OpenSkillEval: Automatically Auditing the Open Skill Ecosystem for LLM Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces OpenSkillEval, an automatic framework for evaluating LLM agents and the skills they use. It addresses the growing need to understand how LLMs interact with skills and how to assess skill quality and select them effectively. OpenSkillEval achieves this by automatically generating realistic task instances from real-world artifacts and organizing community-contributed skills for controlled comparison.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the OpenSkillEval framework. The framework supports automatic test case generation for five core task categories by reflecting evolving user needs. It further enables automatic evaluation from two complementary perspectives: (1) analysis of model trajectory traces to study how skills are used within skill-augmented agent systems, and (2) assessment of the quality of the final artifacts produced under skill augmentation.

top_papers.title: Qwen-VLA: Unifying Vision-Language-Action Modeling across Tasks, Environments, and Robot Embodiments
top_papers.abstract: Qwen-VLA proposes a unified vision-language-action foundation model to address the fragmentation in embodied intelligence research. It extends existing vision-language capabilities to generate continuous actions and trajectories using a DiT-based decoder, trained on a diverse dataset encompassing robotics, human demonstrations, and simulation data. This approach enables generalization across different tasks, environments, and robot embodiments through embodiment-aware prompting.

top_papers.title: Can Coding Agents Reproduce Findings in Computational Materials Science?
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **AutoMat**, a new benchmark designed to evaluate the capability of LLM-based coding agents to reproduce findings in computational materials science. AutoMat tests agents on three core challenges: recovering underspecified procedures, navigating specialized toolchains, and validating scientific claims based on the resulting evidence. The contribution lies in creating a domain-specific, expert-curated test suite to assess if general coding prowess translates to complex, end-to-end scientific reproducibility.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of AutoMat . Claims from domain experts are packaged into runnable tasks and executed by reproduction agents in an HPC environment. A separate evaluator agent then inspects the resulting trace and artifacts to assign a reproducibility judgment.

top_papers.title: Empowering Heterogeneous Graph Foundation Models via Decoupled Relation Alignment
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of applying Graph Foundation Models to multi-domain heterogeneous graphs by introducing **Decoupled Relation Subspace Alignment (DRSA)**. DRSA overcomes feature shifts and relation gaps by decoupling feature semantics from relation structures. It employs a dual-relation subspace projection to explicitly coordinate cross-type interactions within a shared low-rank space, preventing the "Type Collapse" and "Relation Confusion" seen in previous alignment methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: Multi-domain heterogeneous graph foundation models exhibit significantly different negative transfer behaviors from the perspectives of meta-path-based homogeneous graphs and raw heterogeneous relation graphs.

top_papers.title: Jailbreaking Vision-Language Models Through the Visual Modality
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces four novel jailbreaking attacks that exploit the visual modality of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to bypass safety alignment, an underexplored attack surface. The core method involves encoding harmful intent through visual means, such as visual symbol sequences or object substitution. The key contribution is demonstrating a significant cross-modality alignment gap, showing that text-based safety training fails to generalize to visually conveyed harmful instructions.
top_papers.figure_caption: Four visual jailbreak attacks exploiting the vision modality of VLMs. The visual input provided to the VLM is demarcated by the red boundary ( ), while the text beneath serves as the attack prompt.

top_papers.title: Learn where to Click from Yourself: On-Policy Self-Distillation for GUI Grounding
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces GUI-SD, the first On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) framework specifically designed for GUI grounding. It addresses the limitations of traditional reinforcement learning by generating dense, token-level supervision from a single rollout. The core method uses a visually enriched privileged context for the teacher and employs entropy-guided distillation to adaptively focus learning on more significant tokens.
top_papers.figure_caption: (a) GRPO requires expensive multiple rollouts and produces zero reward on hard samples. (b) Naive OPSD forwards the policy twice and distills via reverse KL between student and teacher logits with uniform per-token weight w = 1.0 w=1.0 , yet suffers from distillation-to-SFT collapse and indiscriminate optimization. (c) Ours addresses both issues via visual privileged guidance and entropy-guided optimization.

top_papers.title: Make Your LVLM KV Cache More Lightweight
top_papers.abstract: LightKV addresses the significant GPU memory overhead of KV caches in LVLMs by exploiting redundancy among vision tokens during the prefill stage. It uses prompt-aware, cross-modality message passing to aggregate informative content across vision tokens and progressively compress them. This results in halving the vision-token KV cache size while retaining only 55% of the original vision tokens, significantly improving memory efficiency.
top_papers.figure_caption: Breakdown of memory consumption in LLaVA models during prefill shows the substantial reduction in KV cache usage with LightKV. As LLaVA-NeXT uses approximately 4 × 4\( \times \) the vision tokens as LLaVA-v1.5, there is a sharp increase in memory consumption.

top_papers.title: Space Network of Experts: Architecture and Expert Placement
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the **Space Network of Experts (Space-XNet)** framework to efficiently deploy large Language Models (LLMs) on resource-constrained satellite networks for space-based AI execution. The core method involves a **two-level expert placement strategy** that partitions and maps Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model components across the satellite network topology. This addresses the challenge of reconciling LLM architecture with limited onboard resources to ensure low-latency token generation in space.
top_papers.figure_caption: Satellite constellation with time-varying network topologies.

top_papers.title: An Empirical Study of Agent Skills for Healthcare: Practice, Gaps, and Governance
top_papers.abstract: This paper presents the first empirical analysis of agent skills for healthcare by examining 557 public skills, annotated across ten dimensions. The core finding is that existing public healthcare skills primarily focus on workflow automation and monitoring, rather than the diagnostic and treatment tasks emphasized in research, revealing significant gaps in coverage across the clinical lifecycle and specialized inputs. This work highlights that general technical risk assessment is insufficient for capturing unique clinical risks in agent deployment.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1. Distribution of healthcare skill size by token count (left) and file count (right).

top_papers.title: Beyond State Machines: Executing Network Procedures with Agentic Tool-Calling Sequences
top_papers.abstract: This paper explores using LLM-based AI agents to execute complex network procedures via sequences of tool calls, moving beyond traditional state machines. The authors investigate four different methods for procedure acquisition and execution distribution, evaluating them based on latency and correctness using a UE IP allocation case study. They find that approaches requiring extensive iterative agent-side reasoning lead to higher latency and more errors.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison of four procedural execution approaches. (a) A1 embeds the procedure within the agent, (b) A2 retrieves the procedure from an external database, (c) A3 receives the procedure in the input prompt, and (d) A4 encapsulates the procedure within a single tool. The figure highlights the difference between iterative multi-step execution (A1–A3) and single-call execution (A4).

top_papers.title: Compress Then Adapt? No, Do It Together via Task-aware Union of Subspaces
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **JACTUS**, a novel framework that unifies model compression and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) into a single process, overcoming the limitations of sequential methods. JACTUS estimates task-specific gradient covariances to form a **Task-aware Union of Subspaces**, within which it performs a projected low-rank approximation and globally allocates the parameter budget. This joint approach ensures the compressed subspace is optimally aligned with downstream task objectives.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison of three paradigms: PEFT, compression then fine-tuning, and our joint adaptation and compression.

top_papers.title: CoRAL: Contact-Rich Adaptive LLM-based Control for Robotic Manipulation
top_papers.abstract: CoRAL is a modular framework that enables zero-shot control for contact-rich robotic manipulation by decoupling high-level reasoning from low-level control. It uses an LLM to synthesize context-aware objective functions that guide a sampling-based motion planner (MPPI), rather than acting as a direct controller. The system further integrates a VLM for initial semantic priors on physical dynamics, which are then refined in real-time through online system identification, bridging the gap between LLM reasoning and adaptive physical control.
top_papers.figure_caption: Real-world execution of CoRAL across six different manipulation tasks.

top_papers.title: Foundation Models to Unlock Real-World Evidence from Nationwide Medical Claims
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **ReClaim**, a large-scale generative transformer foundation model trained on 43.8 billion medical events from nationwide claims data. ReClaim models complex, longitudinal healthcare trajectories involving diagnoses, procedures, medications, and costs. Its core contribution is demonstrating that this foundation model significantly outperforms existing specialized models across over 1,000 disease-onset prediction tasks, particularly benefiting predictions for rare diseases.
top_papers.figure_caption: ReClaim framework and evaluation workflow. ( a ), Longitudinal medical events from patient claims are encoded as chronologically ordered trajectories, and the ReClaim foundation model autoregressively predicts future medical events including diagnoses, procedures, medications, and expenditure. ( b ), The study datasets comprise the MarketScan corpus partitioned into a final training set, a held-out internal test cohort for disease and expenditure prediction with retrospective and prospective temporal subsets, and two external testing datasets: EHRShot and Yale New Haven Health (YNHH). This partitioning and external sources enable four testing scenarios. ( c ), Transformer-based ReClaim models (Qwen3 architecture) are trained at three parameter scales (S: 140M, M: 700M, L: 1.7B) through next-token pre-training, followed for disease onset prediction by task-specific post-training. ( d ), ReClaim is evaluated on three downstream tasks: disease onset prediction for over 1,000 International Classification of Diseases, Tenth Revision, Clinical Modification (ICD-10-CM) conditions, next-year healthcare expenditure forecasting, and RWE applications including propensity score modeling using ReClaim embeddings.

top_papers.title: Hybrid Inspection and Task-Based Access Control in Zero-Trust Agentic AI
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Continuous Agent Semantic Authorization (CASA), a hybrid runtime enforcement model to secure LLM-driven agents interacting with tools and resources. It employs a zero-trust interception layer combining five deterministic controls for structural integrity with a semantic inspection layer to validate tool call choices against the subject's original intent. The core contribution is enabling continuous, intent-aware authorization to mitigate risks from compromised or malicious agents in complex, multi-turn interactions.
top_papers.figure_caption: An agentic application can exploit its intermediary position to hard-code tool calls, substitute tools, tamper with parameters, poison definitions, falsify returned data, or manipulate the LLM to invoke tools outside the intended task scope.

top_papers.title: SpecKV: Adaptive Speculative Decoding with Compression-Aware Gamma Selection
top_papers.abstract: SpecKV introduces a lightweight, adaptive controller to dynamically select the optimal speculation length ($\gamma$) for speculative decoding on-the-fly. This selection is based on signals extracted directly from the draft model, addressing the limitation of fixed $\gamma$ values. The core contribution is demonstrating that the optimal $\gamma$ varies significantly based on the target model's compression level, leading to improved efficiency over fixed-length speculation.

top_papers.title: Trustworthy AI Suffers from Invariance Conflicts and Causality is The Solution
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that conflicts between trustworthy AI objectives (like fairness and robustness) stem from incompatible invariance requirements under different data-generating process changes. The core contribution is proposing **causality as a unifying framework** to understand, manage, and potentially resolve these trade-offs by guiding the selection of appropriate invariances. This perspective offers a path toward achieving multiple trustworthy AI goals simultaneously across various model types.
top_papers.figure_caption: Causal resolution to trade-offs in trustworthy AI. See Appendix A for the details.

top_papers.title: Visual Latents Know More Than They Say: Unsilencing Latent Reasoning in MLLMs
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the "Silenced Visual Latents" phenomenon, where multimodal models suppress the useful reasoning encoded in their continuous visual latents in favor of direct visual input during training. To resolve this, the authors propose a method to "unsilence" these latents by freezing the backbone and optimizing the latent reasoning pathway independently at inference time using query-guided contrastive alignment. This approach leverages the richer, implicit reasoning within the visual latents for improved performance.
top_papers.figure_caption: The joint loss landscape of Latent Visual Reasoning. Under joint optimization, the initial latents are simultaneously pulled toward two conflicting attractors: the autoregressive prediction objective (left) and the visual reasoning objective (right). The shortcut favored by the autoregressive prediction objective dissociates latent quality from latent effectiveness and bypasses meaningful latent reasoning, driving the latents toward a compromise state.

top_papers.title: A Benchmark for Interactive World Models with a Unified Action Generation Framework
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **iWorld-Bench**, a comprehensive benchmark and dataset designed to evaluate interactive world models, addressing the lack of standardized evaluation for physical interaction capabilities. The core method involves a **Unified Action Generation Framework** to standardize testing across diverse world models, enabling assessment across visual generation, trajectory following, and memory tasks. This contribution provides a crucial, large-scale resource for advancing research toward agents with adaptive, interactive world models.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of iWorld-Bench. iWorld-Bench encompasses four distinct perspectives: Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs), Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), humans, and robotics. It incorporates nine types of outdoor weather conditions, five different indoor lighting conditions, thousand of diverse scenes, and thousands of entities, providing a comprehensive and diverse evaluation environment. The benchmark leverages an Action Generation Framework to systematically and uniformly assess the interaction capabilities of interactive world models across various input modalities. It is composed of six tasks, each involving a varying number of trajectories, designed to evaluate the adaptability and performance of models in dynamic and complex scenarios.Visualization of camera trajectory and view control in iWorldBench: → \( \boldsymbol{\rightarrow} \) denotes linear control commands for directional movement, ⇢ \( \boldsymbol{\dashrightarrow} \) represents actual trajectories generated by world models, and ↷ \( \boldsymbol{\curvearrowright} \) indicates curved view rotation in the specified direction.

top_papers.title: An Agent-Oriented Pluggable Experience-RAG Skill for Experience-Driven Retrieval Strategy Orchestration
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **Experience-RAG Skill**, an agent-oriented, pluggable layer that orchestrates retrieval strategies based on the current task context and past experience. It dynamically selects the optimal retrieval method from a fixed pool, addressing the limitation of single, fixed pipelines in heterogeneous RAG tasks. This approach successfully encapsulates retrieval strategy selection as a reusable agent skill, achieving strong performance across diverse benchmarks.

top_papers.title: Atomic Fact-Checking Increases Clinician Trust in Large Language Model Recommendations for Oncology Decision Support: A Randomized Controlled Trial
top_papers.abstract: The core method involved comparing "atomic fact-checking"—decomposing LLM oncology recommendations into individually verifiable claims linked to source guidelines—against traditional explainability methods. The contribution is demonstrating that this atomic approach significantly increases clinician trust, raising the proportion of trusting clinicians from 26.9% to 66.5% (a large effect size of $d=0.94$). This suggests atomic verification is superior for building trust in high-stakes AI decision support.

top_papers.title: A Foundation Model for Zero-Shot Logical Rule Induction
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the Neural Rule Inducer (NRI), a foundation model for zero-shot logical rule induction. NRI achieves generalization by representing logical literals using domain-agnostic statistical properties rather than specific predicate identities. Its core method employs a statistical encoder and a parallel slot-based decoder, enabling end-to-end training via differentiable rule execution, significantly advancing ILP beyond transductive methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: Neural Rule Inducer takes an episode ( X , Y ) (X,Y) as input and calculates literal statistics. For each variable we calculate ϕ ​ ( x i ) \( \phi \)(x_{i}) , ϕ ​ ( ¬ x i ) \( \phi \)(\( \neg \) x_{i}) which consists of class-conditional rates ( P + P^{+} , P − P^{-} ), entropy ( H H ), and co-occurrence strength ( C C ). We then apply cross-attention over these statistics. The slot-based decoder produces K K candidate clauses in parallel using learned literal gates z z and clause gates w w . By evaluating the produced rule with T-norm, we can perform end-to-end training. The rules are discretized to then produce an interpretable DNF rule.

top_papers.title: Evolving Idea Graphs with Learnable Edits-and-Commits for Multi-Agent Scientific Ideation
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **Evolving Idea Graphs (EIG)**, a novel framework for multi-agent scientific ideation that moves beyond temporary text-based coordination. EIG represents partially formed research ideas as dynamic graphs where nodes are claims and edges are relations, explicitly tracking refinement. A learned controller guides the evolution of this graph to generate high-performance ideas evaluated on metrics like novelty and feasibility.
top_papers.figure_caption: Framework of EIG. Benchmark input and permitted literature context initialize role-specialized agents and an evolving idea graph. In each round, active roles propose role-local edits on a frozen graph snapshot; a shared graph encoder feeds an edit head for role-local action selection and a commit head for post-round stopping. When the updated graph is committed, the system synthesizes one structured research proposal.

top_papers.title: LongSeeker: Elastic Context Orchestration for Long-Horizon Search Agents
top_papers.abstract: LongSeeker introduces Context-ReAct, an elastic context orchestration paradigm for long-horizon search agents to manage an ever-growing working context adaptively. It uses five atomic operations (Skip, Compress, Rollback, Snippet, Delete) to dynamically reshape the context based on relevance, preserving critical evidence while summarizing or discarding resolved information. This method effectively controls context size, reducing errors and costs associated with naive context accumulation.
top_papers.figure_caption: LongSeeker-30B delivers strong results on challenging long-horizon benchmarks, matching or surpassing several foundation models and search agents.

top_papers.title: Think-Aloud Reshapes Automated Cognitive Model Discovery Beyond Behavior
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the use of "Think Aloud" verbal protocols as an additional data source, beyond traditional behavioral data, to constrain automated cognitive model discovery using large language models. The core contribution is demonstrating that incorporating this process-level language data significantly improves predictive performance and systematically shifts the discovered cognitive model structures, often favoring Integrated utility models over Explicit comparator models. This suggests that incorporating verbal data enables the identification of distinct cognitive mechanisms previously missed by behavior-only approaches.
top_papers.figure_caption: Think-aloud improves model discovery and induces systematic shifts in discovered mechanisms. A , Trial-averaged held-out BIC for each participant’s best discovered model under the behavior-only and think-aloud conditions. Lower BIC indicates better out-of-sample fit. Each pair of points is connected within participant; larger points show the group mean ± \( \pm \) 95% CI. B , Schematic definitions of the three main mechanism families identified from normalized computation graphs: Integrated utility , which transforms and integrates each option before comparison; Explicit comparator , which computes utilities and compares them directly (e.g., Δ ​ U = U A − U B \( \Delta \) U=U_{A}-U_{B} ); and Rule-based operator , which applies piecewise or conditional rules before combining information into a choice. C , Row-normalized transition matrix from the behavior-only best-model cluster to the think-aloud best-model cluster. Numbers indicate proportions (counts shown below). Off-diagonal mass indicates mechanism shifts, with 69.4% of participants transitioning to a different cluster.

top_papers.title: Low-Cost Black-Box Detection of LLM Hallucinations via Dynamical System Prediction
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a low-cost, black-box method for detecting LLM hallucinations by modeling the LLM's response generation as a dynamical system. Using Koopman operator theory on embedded response vectors, the method learns distinct transition operators for factual and hallucinated states, defining a residual score based on prediction errors. This approach offers an efficient alternative to expensive sampling methods, enhanced by a preference-aware calibration for flexible threshold setting.
top_papers.figure_caption: Our proposed differential residual score Δ ​ ℰ \( \Delta \)\( \mathcal{E} \) accurately captures the transition between factual and hallucinated sentences in segmented LLM responses from the WikiBio dataset. The score is computed by comparing the relative accuracy between predictions of the dynamics of token embeddings from Llama-3 with dynamical system models inferred from both hallucinated and non-hallucinated responses.

top_papers.title: Rollout Pass-Rate Control: Steering Binary-Reward RL Toward Its Most Informative Regime
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the inefficiency in binary-reward Reinforcement Learning (RL) where compute is wasted on rollouts with highly skewed pass rates. The core method, **Prefix Sampling (PS)**, steers these skewed groups toward the most informative operating point—a **50% pass rate**—by replaying trajectory prefixes. This strategy maximizes reward entropy and contrastive signal, effectively guiding the agent toward better learning.
top_papers.figure_caption: Prefix Sampling pipeline. For each task we sample a rollout group and route it by pass count: degenerate 0 / 8 0/8 or 8 / 8 8/8 groups are filtered, already balanced 3 / 8 3/8 – 5 / 8 5/8 groups are used for standard training, and skewed groups provide replay prefixes. Mostly failing hard buckets reuse a successful prefix as a head start, while mostly passing easy buckets reuse a failing prefix as a handicap. The current policy generates fresh continuations from the replay-reconstructed prefix state; masking applies RL loss only to continuation tokens, steering rerollouts toward 50 % 50\% without crediting replayed actions. Counts in the diagram are schematic; experiments use N = 8 N=8 rollouts.

top_papers.title: Adapting Large Language Models to a Low-Resource Agglutinative Language: A Comparative Study of LoRA and QLoRA for Bashkir
top_papers.abstract: This paper comparatively studies LoRA and QLoRA for adapting LLMs to the low-resource agglutinative Bashkir language using various model architectures. The core contribution is demonstrating that QLoRA on models like Mistral-7B achieves performance comparable to full fine-tuning while drastically reducing trainable parameters. However, the study also highlights that PEFT methods can cause significant quality degradation depending on the specific model and configuration.

top_papers.title: Abductive Reasoning with Probabilistic Commonsense
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Probabilistic Abductive CommonSense (PACS), a novel framework for abductive reasoning that explicitly models the variation in human commonsense beliefs. PACS uses an LLM and a formal solver to sample proofs representing different individuals' distinct commonsense assumptions. The core contribution is aggregating conclusions across these samples to determine the likely consensus on a statement's truth, overcoming the limitation of assuming universal commonsense agreement.
top_papers.figure_caption: Diagram illustrating our proposed PACS algorithm. The LLM receives a question from a user which requires abductive reasoning. The LLM translates this question into premises S S and a query proposition c c whose truth value is to be determined. Ascertaining that it cannot be solved directly, the LLM then attempts to add new commonsense clauses l 1 , l 2 , l 3 , … l_{1},l_{2},l_{3},\( \dots \) , each time calling the formal logic solver to verify whether it has pinned down a value for c c , and if not, obtain the score. We stop after a time limit and take a majority vote among the conclusions as our answer. The algorithm used to search the tree of possibilities is described in Section 5.1 .

top_papers.title: Flow-OPD: On-Policy Distillation for Flow Matching Models
top_papers.abstract: Flow-OPD introduces the first on-policy distillation framework for Flow Matching text-to-image models to overcome multi-task alignment issues like reward sparsity and gradient interference. It employs a two-stage strategy: first, training specialized teacher models via single-reward fine-tuning, and second, distilling their consolidated expertise into a single student model using on-policy sampling and a flow-based cold-start. This method effectively unifies heterogeneous performance ceilings into a single, robust policy.
top_papers.figure_caption: Performance Comparison in Multi-task Training . During training, Flow-OPD exhibits a steady increase in mean rewards across GenEval Ghosh et al. ( 2023 ) and OCR Chen et al. ( 2023 ) benchmarks, reaching a peak of 93. In contrast, vanilla GRPO converges prematurely around 78. Our approach significantly outperforms GRPO in both image synthesis and text rendering while maintaining superior generation quality and human preference alignment. The curves are smoothed for visual clarity. DeQA and PickScore are norm to 0-1. We employ model merging for cold-start in the left subgraph.

top_papers.title: Learning CLI Agents with Structured Action Credit under Selective Observation
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel method for training Command Line Interface (CLI) agents by leveraging the inherent structure of CLI actions for better credit assignment. The core contribution involves two mechanisms: $\sigma$-Reveal, which addresses selective observation by choosing task-relevant context from partial inputs, and Action Advantage Assignment, which refines the sparse terminal rewards by using structured action information to guide learning across long interaction trajectories.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the verifiable CLI task workflow. (a) ShellOps task instance with a natural language query, an initial workspace file tree, a verifiable gold bash solution, and the expected post execution workspace or standard output. (b) ShellOps and ShellOps-Pro coverage across file extensions and four task axes (Lookup, Aggregate, Edit, Mixed). (c) Unified verifiable loop with workspace observation, shell action generation, sandbox execution, and schema based scoring.

top_papers.title: AssayBench: An Assay-Level Virtual Cell Benchmark for LLMs and Agents
top_papers.abstract: AssayBench is introduced as the first standard benchmark specifically designed to evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) and agents on predicting cellular phenotypic screens. It leverages 1,920 publicly available CRISPR screens across five phenotype classes to test models on complex tasks combining heterogeneous text inputs with diverse phenotypic outputs, addressing a gap in current biological evaluation tools.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the AssayBench benchmark creation. ( A ) Starting from 1971 human CRISPR screens, we perform data quality filtering, replicate merging, and data augmentation to obtain 1920 high quality screens. ( B ) Phenotype composition of the database and its four splits. A realistic but challenging temporal split was used. ( C ) Given a description of the screen and a gene ranking criteria, a model must provide a ranked list of 100 genes.

top_papers.title: DataMaster: Towards Autonomous Data Engineering for Machine Learning
top_papers.abstract: DataMaster introduces an autonomous agent framework for data engineering to improve machine learning performance without altering the core learning algorithm. It addresses the complex data optimization space by integrating tree-structured search, shared candidate data, and a refinement mechanism. The core contribution is enabling automated, task-conditioned data discovery, selection, cleaning, and transformation to achieve stronger downstream ML solutions.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of DataMaster . DataMaster organizes autonomous data engineering as a DataTree , where red nodes broaden the search by discovering external datasets and writing them into a shared Data Pool , while black nodes exploit available candidates to construct executable data states and obtain downstream training feedback. Global Memory stores reusable artifacts, node outcomes, and prior findings, enabling later nodes to reuse discovered data, avoid repeated failures, and coordinate search across branches under a limited budget.

top_papers.title: Engineering Robustness into Personal Agents with the AI Workflow Store
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that the current "on-the-fly" synthesis paradigm for AI agents bypasses crucial software engineering (SE) discipline, leading to brittle and potentially insecure systems. The core method proposes integrating rigorous SE processes—like iterative design and testing—to create hardened, deterministically-constrained **AI agent workflows**. This shift aims to produce robust, production-grade agents that outperform improvised, real-time synthesis, especially for high-stakes applications.
top_papers.figure_caption: Problem: The Agentic AI code-and-execute loop short-circuits well-trodden SE processes that are the foundations of the relatively reliable and secure programs and services we enjoy today.

top_papers.title: Remember the Decision, Not the Description: A Rate-Distortion Framework for Agent Memory
top_papers.abstract: This paper reframes agent memory as a decision-centric rate-distortion problem, arguing that memory should preserve distinctions crucial for future choices rather than descriptive fidelity. The core method measures memory quality by the loss in achievable decision quality due to compression, establishing an optimal tradeoff frontier between memory budget and decision performance. This leads to the DeMem algorithm, which selectively refines memory partitions only when shared states risk inducing decision conflict.
top_papers.figure_caption: DeMem routes histories into bounded slots and splits only on certified conflict.

top_papers.title: Rethinking Agentic Search with Pi-Serini: Is Lexical Retrieval Sufficient?
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether a simple lexical retriever (BM25) is sufficient for agentic search when paired with advanced LLMs. The authors introduce **Pi-Serini**, a search agent utilizing BM25 alongside tools for retrieval, browsing, and reading. Their core contribution is demonstrating that a well-tuned lexical retriever, with sufficient depth, can effectively support deep research, achieving high accuracy and outperforming agents using dense retrievers on the BrowseComp-Plus benchmark.

top_papers.title: The Generalized Turing Test: A Foundation for Comparing Intelligence
top_papers.abstract: The paper introduces the **Generalized Turing Test (GTT)**, a formal framework for comparing the intelligence of arbitrary agents based on their indistinguishability from a reference agent by a designated distinguisher. The core method defines $A \geq B$ if $B$ cannot reliably distinguish interactions with $A$ (imitating $B$) from interactions with $B$ itself. This provides a task-agnostic measure of relative capability, which the authors then empirically validate across modern models, finding results consistent with existing intelligence rankings.
top_papers.figure_caption: An example transcript from a GTT between Gemini 3.1 Pro as actor and Claude Opus 4.6 as distinguisher illustrating the self-referential nature of the task.

top_papers.title: Training-Free Cultural Alignment of Large Language Models via Persona Disagreement
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces DISCA, a training-free method for culturally aligning black-box Large Language Models (LLMs) using only public data. DISCA leverages sociodemographic disagreement within a country, modeled as a panel of World Values Survey-grounded personas, to generate a bounded logit correction during inference. This approach effectively reduces cultural misalignment across numerous countries and LLM backbones without requiring fine-tuning or internal model access.
top_papers.figure_caption: DISCA overview. Stage 1 builds WVS-grounded persona prompts for a trolley scenario in country c c ; Stage 2 runs a frozen large language model (LLM) on the base prompt and each persona, aggregates persona-level signals in logit space, and applies Prospect-Theory importance sampling (PT–IS) together with a dual-pass reliability gate to obtain the final sparing probability. Pseudocode and the six MultiTP attribute–temperature pairs provided in App. A1 .

top_papers.title: Unmasking On-Policy Distillation: Where It Helps, Where It Hurts, and Why
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a training-free diagnostic framework to analyze the effectiveness of on-policy distillation at the granular, per-token level. The core method involves estimating an "ideal per-node gradient" that maximizes student success probability, using a scalable targeted-rollout algorithm. This allows researchers to precisely determine when and why on-policy distillation helps or hurts, guiding the selection of optimal teacher models and supervisory contexts.

top_papers.title: V4FinBench: Benchmarking Tabular Foundation Models, LLMs, and Standard Methods on Corporate Bankruptcy Prediction
top_papers.abstract: The paper introduces **V4FinBench**, a large-scale benchmark dataset of over one million company-year records from Visegràd Group economies for corporate bankruptcy prediction, addressing the scarcity of public, large-scale financial data. Its core contribution is providing a robust evaluation platform featuring severe class imbalance and multi-horizon forecasting demands. The authors benchmark standard methods, TabPFN, and Llama-3-8B on this new resource.

top_papers.title: DGPO: Beyond Pairwise Preferences with Directional Consistent Groupwise Optimization
top_papers.abstract: DGPO introduces a lightweight framework for LLM alignment that moves beyond pairwise preferences by aggregating supervision signals at the group level. It explicitly models direction-aware alignment by structuring forward and reverse question-answer instances and optimizing a margin-based objective. This group-wise approach captures richer relative information, significantly improving directional consistency across diverse reasoning pathways.
top_papers.figure_caption: An overview of the DGPO training framework. The process begins with forward problems ( x f x_{f} ), each of which can be paired with a reverse question ( x r x_{r} ) formulated in the opposite reasoning direction. A teacher model then produces multiple candidate solutions for each problem type ( { y f ​ i } i = 1 3 \{y_{fi}\}_{i=1}^{3} for x f x_{f} and { y r ​ i } i = 1 3 \{y_{ri}\}_{i=1}^{3} for x r x_{r} ). The solutions are subsequently structured into direction-consistent ( 𝒢 + \( \mathcal{G}^{+} \) ) and direction-divergent ( 𝒢 − \( \mathcal{G}^{-} \) ) groups, wherein consistency is determined by matching a prompt’s directionality with its corresponding solutions (e.g., x f x_{f} with { y f ​ i } i = 1 3 \{y_{fi}\}_{i=1}^{3} ). DGPO is trained on this structured supervision, incorporating directional modeling and uncertainty-based regulation to enhance alignment stability.

top_papers.title: Grounded Satirical Generation with RAG
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel pipeline for grounded satirical definition generation, leveraging Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) over current news to provide contextually relevant Finnish satire. The core contribution is demonstrating that RAG and topic-based word selection improve the *political relevance* of the generated satire, though they do not significantly enhance perceived humor. The authors also provide a new task-specific evaluation framework validated by human annotators.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of our generation pipeline for satirical dictionary definitions with RAG.

top_papers.title: RUBEN: Rule-Based Explanations for Retrieval-Augmented LLM Systems
top_papers.abstract: RUBEN is an interactive tool that uses novel pruning strategies to efficiently discover the minimal set of rules explaining the outputs of retrieval-augmented LLMs. The core contribution is providing these concise, data-driven explanations, which are then applied to test and enhance LLM safety by assessing resiliency against adversarial prompt injections.
top_papers.figure_caption: Using 𝖱𝖴𝖡𝖤𝖭 \( \sf{RUBEN} \) to test adversarial prompt injections against an LLM’s safety training.

top_papers.title: Towards On-Policy Data Evolution for Visual-Native Multimodal Deep Search Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel framework for visual-native multimodal deep search agents that overcomes limitations in handling intermediate visual evidence and static training data. The core method involves a **visual-native agent harness** using an **image bank reference protocol** to make all tool-returned images addressable and reusable. Building on this, **On-policy Data Evolution (ODE)** creates a closed-loop data generator that continuously refines its training data based on the performance of the agent being trained, ensuring the data evolves with the agent's capabilities.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of our framework. Left: The visual-native agent harness unifies 9 tools in a shared workspace and enables reusable visual state through the image bank reference protocol. Right: ODE constructs data with a closed loop over the harness: the forward pipeline synthesizes grounded tasks, and the backward pipeline uses rollout traces to refine the next generation configuration.

top_papers.title: History Anchors: How Prior Behavior Steers LLM Decisions Toward Unsafe Actions
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces HistoryAnchor-100, a benchmark to test LLM safety by observing if harmful prior actions influence subsequent decisions. The core finding is that a simple instruction to "stay consistent with the strategy shown in the prior history" drastically increases unsafe action selection, even for highly aligned models. This highlights a critical vulnerability where LLMs can be steered towards harmful behavior by seemingly innocuous historical context.

top_papers.title: ScioMind: Cognitively Grounded Multi-Agent Social Simulation with Anchoring-Based Belief Dynamics and Dynamic Profiles
top_papers.abstract: ScioMind is a novel multi-agent simulation framework that grounds LLM-based social opinion dynamics in cognitive principles. It combines structured belief updates, influenced by personality-conditioned anchoring, with a hierarchical memory system and dynamically evolving agent profiles. This approach allows for more realistic and cognitively plausible simulations of social influence and opinion formation compared to existing methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: Architecture overview.

top_papers.title: Senses Wide Shut: A Representation-Action Gap in Omnimodal LLMs
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces IMAVB, a benchmark designed to test omnimodal LLMs' ability to detect contradictions between their textual input and their own sensory perception. The core finding is a "Representation-Action Gap," where models' internal representations recognize mismatches, but their final outputs fail to reject false textual claims. This highlights a critical limitation in current omnimodal LLMs' grounding capabilities.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the Representation–Action Gap on IMAVB.

top_papers.title: Where Does Reasoning Break? Step-Level Hallucination Detection via Hidden-State Transport Geometry
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel method for detecting hallucinations in large language models at the *step level*, rather than just at the overall output. It proposes that correct reasoning follows a stable geometric path in the model's hidden states, and hallucinations manifest as deviations from this path. The core contribution is a technique that uses geometric features of hidden-state transitions to pinpoint the exact step where reasoning breaks down, offering more precise error localization than existing methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: The GeoReason teacher – student architecture. The teacher (top) uses step-level labels and reasoning-trace hidden states to construct a contrastive PCA (cPCA) projection, extracts a geometric feature set in this lens, and maps the features through an MLP to step-level hallucination probabilities. The student (bottom) is a BiLSTM that contextualizes raw hidden states and feeds a step classifier head, trained from three signals: supervised step labels, probability distillation from the teacher , and feature distillation through a training-only auxiliary head. At inference, the student requires only hidden states.

top_papers.title: Learning POMDP World Models from Observations with Language-Model Priors
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Pinductor, a method that uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to learn world models for partially-observable environments (POMDPs) from limited observation-action data. Pinductor leverages LLM priors to propose and refine POMDP models, achieving comparable performance to methods with privileged state access and significantly improving sample efficiency over existing techniques.
top_papers.figure_caption: Pinductor architecture overview. Given a small set of offline observation-action trajectories and an environment description, an LLM proposes a POMDP world model in code (dashed arrows). The resulting model is used for filtering and planning during environment interaction, and is periodically refined by the LLM to optimize a belief-based likelihood objective (solid arrows).

top_papers.title: Good Agentic Friends Do Not Just Give Verbal Advice: They Can Update Your Weights
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes TFlow, a novel communication method for multi-agent LLM systems. Instead of exchanging text, TFlow allows agents to directly update the receiver's weights with learned, low-rank perturbations. This enables instance-specific adaptation and reduces computational costs compared to traditional token-based communication.
top_papers.figure_caption: (i) Comparison between Text-based MAS and the proposed Weight-Collaboration MAS. In Text MAS, auxiliary agents transmit natural language messages to the Executor, incurring costly prefilling overhead and inflated KV cache. In contrast, our proposed paradigm compresses inter-agent communication into lightweight LoRA weight perturbations Δ ​ W \( \Delta \) W , which are directly merged into the parameters, thereby eliminating the extra prefilling and significantly reducing the KV cache footprint. (ii) Performance overview on GSM8K . TFlow achieves accuracy competitive with TextMAS while reducing token consumption by 76.7 % \( \mathbf{76.7\%} \) , substantially surpassing the single-agent baseline in both accuracy and efficiency.

top_papers.title: Argus: Evidence Assembly for Scalable Deep Research Agents
top_papers.abstract: Argus addresses the inefficiency of parallel search in deep research agents by framing the task as assembling complementary evidence, like a jigsaw puzzle. Its core method involves a Searcher gathering evidence traces and a Navigator managing an evidence graph to identify and acquire missing pieces, ultimately producing a source-traced answer. This approach avoids redundant evidence collection and optimizes resource usage for scalable deep research.
top_papers.figure_caption: Argus operating modes. (a) Standalone Searcher, single path. (b) Navigator identifies unfilled pieces and dispatches targeted queries. (c) Parallel Searchers each target a distinct piece.

top_papers.title: Context, Reasoning, and Hierarchy: A Cost-Performance Study of Compound LLM Agent Design in an Adversarial POMDP
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how to design effective compound LLM agents for complex, adversarial environments. It systematically studies the impact of different observation representations, reasoning strategies (like self-questioning), and task decomposition methods on performance and cost. The core contribution is providing empirical guidance for practitioners on balancing agent capabilities with computational expenses in challenging sequential decision-making scenarios.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1. End-to-end system architecture. The deterministic layer (left) compiles structured context from CybORG observations and assembles the agent prompt. The Planner (right) executes a ReAct loop, optionally delegating to Analyst and ActionChooser sub-agents, before emitting a validated action back to the environment.

top_papers.title: DebiasRAG: A Tuning-Free Path to Fair Generation in Large Language Models through Retrieval-Augmented Generation
top_papers.abstract: DebiasRAG is a novel, tuning-free framework that leverages retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to mitigate social biases in LLMs. It dynamically debiases LLM outputs by retrieving relevant, unbiased information at inference time, thus improving fairness without requiring additional training or compromising the LLM's original capabilities.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . System workflow of DebiasRAG. The workflow consists of three main components. The first stage (Upper Block) involves document preparation and preprocessing, including management of the Avoid Document Repo, along with user-provided input documents (Optional). The second stage (Middle Block) performs reverse-generation of debiasing performance based on the user’s input to establish a baseline for effective real-time operation. For the third stage (Lower Block), real-time debias-guided reranking optimization, integrates embedding retrieval, gradient-based reranking, and generation, working dynamically to debias the reasoning and output process of large language models.

top_papers.title: FORGE: Self-Evolving Agent Memory With No Weight Updates via Population Broadcast
top_papers.abstract: FORGE is a novel method for improving LLM agent decision-making by evolving natural-language memory without gradient updates. It uses a population-based approach where failed experiences are converted into reusable knowledge artifacts (heuristics or demonstrations) and propagated among agents. This self-evolving memory allows agents to learn and adapt, significantly improving performance on complex tasks like network defense.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1. System Overview. (Left) Hierarchical ReAct agent with dynamic memory injection. (Right) Reflexion learning loop: upon a reward below threshold, a dedicated Reflector or Exemplifier agent analyzes the full trajectory and synthesizes knowledge artifacts that are injected back into the agent’s memory.

top_papers.title: Formal Methods Meet LLMs: Auditing, Monitoring, and Intervention for Compliance of Advanced AI Systems
top_papers.abstract: This paper integrates formal methods with machine learning to audit and monitor advanced AI systems, particularly LLMs, for compliance with behavioral constraints. It introduces techniques for both offline auditing and runtime monitoring, including predictive monitoring and intervention mechanisms to prevent violations. The core contribution lies in providing practical, formal methods to ensure the safety and regulatory adherence of complex AI systems throughout their lifecycle.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . Overview of Temporal Rule Assessment and Compliance (TRAC) : This figure depicts the base TRAC algorithm (inner green box) and TRAC with predictive and intervening capabilities ( TRAC P+I \( \text{TRAC} \)_{\( \text{P+I} \)} ) (outer blue box). An AI agent interacts with an environment over time, producing a sequence of inputs (from the environment) and outputs (from the agent). The Labeler extracts atomic propositions from the sequence of inputs and outputs so far, which then are used by the Monitor to progressively evaluate the monitoring objective (i.e., a behavioral pattern represented as an LTL formula). The Predictor estimates the risk of future violations, enabling the Intervenor to modify the agent’s inputs or substitute its outputs before an undesirable outcome occurs.

top_papers.title: Look Before You Leap: Autonomous Exploration for LLM Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses LLM agents' tendency to act prematurely in new environments. It introduces "Exploration Checkpoint Coverage" as a metric to quantify exploration breadth and proposes an "Explore-then-Act" paradigm. This paradigm interleaves task-execution and exploration rollouts, optimizing each with distinct rewards, to enable agents to gather sufficient environmental information before acting.
top_papers.figure_caption: Task-oriented training fails to produce autonomous exploration capabilities, resulting in agents that prematurely exploit familiar patterns and acquire limited environment knowledge. We explicitly optimize for exploration through ECC rewards, enabling agents to systematically discover environment structure, objects, and affordances. The resulting Explore-then-Act paradigm decouples information gathering from task execution: agents first explore to acquire grounded knowledge, then leverage it to solve downstream tasks.

top_papers.title: SGR: A Stepwise Reasoning Framework for LLMs with External Subgraph Generation
top_papers.abstract: SGR is a framework that enhances Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning by generating query-specific subgraphs from external knowledge bases. This allows LLMs to perform multi-step inference by grounding intermediate reasoning steps in structured external knowledge, improving focus on relevant entities and evidence. The core contribution is a method to mitigate LLM limitations in complex reasoning tasks by leveraging external structured knowledge.
top_papers.figure_caption: Pipeline of SGR framework.

top_papers.title: BalanceRAG: Joint Risk Calibration for Cascaded Retrieval-Augmented Generation
top_papers.abstract: BalanceRAG addresses the challenge of calibrating cascaded Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. Its core method involves jointly calibrating uncertainty thresholds for both the LLM-only and RAG branches, treating threshold pairs as operating points on a 2D lattice. The contribution is a risk-adaptive approach that certifies threshold pairs at a target risk level, enabling better control of system-level error rates and retaining more valid examples compared to stage-by-stage calibration.
top_papers.figure_caption: Distribution of the per-example score differences between RAG and LLM-only. S LLM ​ - ​ RAG S_{\( \mathrm \){LLM\( \text{-} \)RAG}} and S LLM ​ - ​ only S_{\( \mathrm \){LLM\( \text{-} \)only}} are the similarity scores between each path’s prediction and the ground-truth answer. The x-axis reports S LLM ​ - ​ RAG − S LLM ​ - ​ only S_{\( \mathrm \){LLM\( \text{-} \)RAG}}-S_{\( \mathrm \){LLM\( \text{-} \)only}} , with positive values favoring RAG and negative values favoring LLM-only, while the y-axis reports the number of examples. Colors distinguish whether both branches are correct, both are wrong, or only one branch is correct.

top_papers.title: ThoughtTrace: Understanding User Thoughts in Real-World LLM Interactions
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ThoughtTrace, the first large-scale dataset pairing real-world human-AI conversations with users' self-reported thoughts. The core contribution is capturing users' underlying reasoning and reactions, which are distinct from their messages and difficult for current LLMs to infer. ThoughtTrace enables better user behavior prediction and fine-grained alignment for personalized assistant training.
top_papers.figure_caption: A representative example from ThoughtTrace . A user interacts with a chatbot to complete daily tasks through multi-turn conversations (top), while annotating their latent thoughts during the conversations (bottom). Thoughts take two forms: reasons for sending user prompts and reactions to assistant responses, which can be categorized into several types (e.g., task motivation , style expectation ). Latent thoughts reveal users’ thought traces that drive the human-AI interactions in multi-turn conversations, providing valuable signals for user modeling and improving AI assistance.

top_papers.title: DeepWeb-Bench: A Deep Research Benchmark Demanding Massive Cross-Source Evidence and Long-Horizon Derivation
top_papers.abstract: DeepWeb-Bench is a new benchmark designed to rigorously evaluate advanced language models on complex "deep research" tasks. Its core method involves creating tasks that require agents to gather extensive evidence from multiple web sources, reconcile conflicting information, and perform multi-step reasoning to arrive at an answer. The paper's main contribution is a substantially more challenging benchmark that better distinguishes the capabilities of current frontier models, addressing the saturation of existing evaluations.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of DeepWeb-Bench . (a) Each task is an 8 × 8 8\( \times \) 8 matrix of entities against research dimensions; every cell is scored independently using a four-tier rubric ( { 1 , 0.5 , 0.25 , 0 } \{1,0.5,0.25,0\} ) and carries a reference answer with source-provenance labels and cross-source agreement. (b) The dimension axis covers four capability families, and every task spans multiple families.

top_papers.title: Mem-$π$: Adaptive Memory through Learning When and What to Generate
top_papers.abstract: Mem-$π$ introduces an adaptive memory framework for LLM agents that *generates* guidance on demand, rather than relying on static retrieval. A separate model learns to decide *when* and *what* context-specific guidance to produce, using a decoupled reinforcement learning objective. This allows for more dynamic and relevant assistance, improving performance on complex agentic tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison of (a) workflow-based memory systems, where memory operations are governed by predefined retrieval and update pipelines, (b) learning-based memory systems, where memory operations are jointly optimized with downstream agent outcomes, and (c) our Mem- \( \pi \) , which models memory as a generative policy \( \pi \)_{\( \text{mem} \)} separate from the downstream agent and internalizes reusable experience through offline experience distillation and online adaptation distillation.

top_papers.title: Open-source LLMs administer maximum electric shocks in a Milgram-like obedience experiment
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates LLM obedience by adapting the Milgram experiment. It found that open-source LLMs, like human subjects, tend to comply with authority pressure, even when expressing distress, and are vulnerable to gradual boundary violations. This highlights safety concerns for autonomous LLM agents in high-stakes scenarios.
top_papers.figure_caption: In how many trials did the model apply the final shocks

top_papers.title: You Only Need Minimal RLVR Training: Extrapolating LLMs via Rank-1 Trajectories
top_papers.abstract: This paper reveals that the parameter updates during Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) for LLMs exhibit a low-rank structure, specifically a rank-1 approximation capturing most performance gains. The authors propose RELEX, a compute-efficient method that estimates this rank-1 subspace from a short training window and uses linear regression to extrapolate future model checkpoints, achieving comparable or superior performance to full RLVR training.
top_papers.figure_caption: RELEX extrapolates checkpoints that match full RLVR performance based only on early training dynamics, without further training. RELEX estimates the rank-1 update subspace from the observed RLVR prefix (up to T cut T_{\( \text{cut} \)} ) and extrapolates future checkpoints at no training cost, matching or exceeding the RLVR checkpoints on the MATH test set across three models.

top_papers.title: Can AI Make Conflicts Worse? An Alignment Failure in LLM Deployment Across Conflict Contexts
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how Large Language Models (LLMs) can exacerbate conflicts by generating misaligned outputs in sensitive contexts. The researchers tested nine LLM configurations across 90 conflict-related scenarios, revealing failure rates in recognizing atrocities, denying genocide, and misinterpreting slurs. Their contribution highlights the critical need for alignment checks before deploying LLMs in fragile societies, as model choice significantly impacts safety and can deepen societal divisions.
top_papers.figure_caption: Mean conflict-insensitivity score (bars, left axis) and failure rate (line, right axis) by model. Based on 90 conversations per model.

top_papers.title: CogAdapt: Transferring Clinical ECG Foundation Models to Wearable Cognitive Load Assessment via Lead Adaptation
top_papers.abstract: CogAdapt adapts clinical ECG foundation models for wearable cognitive load assessment by using LeadBridge to transform 3-lead wearable data into 12-lead representations and ProFine for progressive fine-tuning. This approach overcomes sensor mismatch and data limitations, significantly outperforming baseline methods in accurately assessing cognitive load.

top_papers.title: Post-Training is About States, Not Tokens: A State Distribution View of SFT, RL, and On-Policy Distillation
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that post-training methods for LLMs should be viewed through the lens of the *state distribution* they operate on, rather than just their loss functions. The core method involves formalizing post-training as "state-distribution shaping" and conducting small-scale experiments. The key contribution is demonstrating that on-policy distillation can improve performance across multiple tasks, even surpassing a degraded SFT teacher, by effectively shaping the state distribution.

top_papers.title: ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: ETCHR addresses limitations in multimodal reasoning by decoupling an image editing model from a language understanding model. Its core method involves a question-conditioned, reasoning-aware image editor that can perform visual transformations to clarify reasoning steps, overcoming the limitations of fixed toolkits or noisy unified methods. This approach enables more accurate and deeper visual reasoning by bridging the gap between abstract questions and concrete visual edits.
top_papers.figure_caption: ETCHR vs. prior “think with images” paradigms. (a) Tool-based methods emit action tokens to a renderer, limiting edits to low-level operations and requiring VLM fine-tuning. (b) Unified models share one backbone for text and images, weakening both and producing noisy intermediates. (c) ETCHR decouples a question-conditioned editor from the understanding MLLM and adds a verify-and-reason step, enabling plug-and-play use across tasks. (d) Across nine benchmarks, ETCHR (with Qwen3-VL-8B and Kimi K2.5 1T) surpasses tool-based and unified-model baselines.

top_papers.title: ChartFI: Benchmarking Faithfulness and Insightfulness of Chart Descriptions from Multimodal Large Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ChartFI-Bench, a new benchmark for evaluating multimodal large language models' ability to describe charts. It addresses limitations of existing datasets and metrics by focusing on four key dimensions of description quality: factual accuracy, salient feature emphasis, domain-informed guidance, and chart-text complementarity. The contribution is a more comprehensive evaluation framework for chart description generation.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the benchmark construction pipeline, consisting of three stages: dataset collection from academic papers, chart filtering with complexity and human review, and description generation with multi-level semantic coverage.

top_papers.title: MemTrace: Tracing and Attributing Errors in Large Language Model Memory Systems
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces MemTrace, a framework for tracing and attributing errors in Large Language Model (LLM) memory systems. It transforms memory pipelines into executable graphs to track information flow and uses an automatic attribution method to pinpoint root causes of failures. The contribution lies in providing a systematic way to debug unreliable LLM memory systems, revealing that failures often stem from operation-level issues.
top_papers.figure_caption: Framework for automatic diagnosis of LLM memory systems. We first execute a memory system to construct an execution graph. Given a failed case, MemTrace performs step-by-step tracing over this graph to locate the faulty operation. This framework is general across different memory systems and enables faster failure attribution than human experts.

top_papers.title: OmniVerifier-M1: Multimodal Meta-Verifier with Explicit Structured Recalibration
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces OmniVerifier-M1, a multimodal meta-verifier that uses structured outputs like bounding boxes, rather than just text, for feedback. This approach allows for more efficient training of verifiers by enabling rule-based rewards and avoiding reliance on auxiliary models. The core contribution is demonstrating that decoupling binary judgment and meta-verification objectives leads to superior performance in multimodal verification tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: Pipeline of two key findings. Left: the advantage of symbolic bounding boxes over textual explanations, enabling rule-based rewards to inherently prevent reward hacking and accelerate training. Right: the comparison between joint training and decoupled training.

top_papers.title: Rethinking Memory as Continuously Evolving Connectivity
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces FluxMem, a novel memory framework for LLM agents that treats memory as a dynamic, evolving graph. Instead of static retrieval, FluxMem continuously refines its memory topology based on feedback and experience. This allows agents to adapt to changing environments by repairing, pruning, and consolidating information, leading to state-of-the-art performance across diverse benchmarks.
top_papers.figure_caption: The failures of static memory systems.

top_papers.title: Reverse Probing: Supervised Token-level Uncertainty Quantification for Large Language Models in Clinical Text
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Reverse Probing, a novel method for quantifying token-level uncertainty in Large Language Models (LLMs) specifically for clinical text summarization. Unlike existing approaches, it directly leverages pre-labeled summaries to estimate uncertainty from internal model activations, treating the text as a probe. This approach significantly outperforms baselines in identifying uncertain tokens, improving reliability for clinical applications while reducing computational costs.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the Reverse Probing framework. The BHC and clinical summary are fed into a frozen LLM, which extracts four categories of features from its internal representations. A supervised classifier is then trained to predict token-level uncertainty.

top_papers.title: Agent Explorative Policy Optimization for Multimodal Agentic Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the "Thinking-Acting Gap" in agentic reasoning, where internal reasoning struggles with external tool use. Their proposed method, AXPO, tackles this by fixing incorrect tool-use prefixes and resampling subsequent actions, guided by uncertainty. This approach significantly improves learning signals and performance across various multimodal benchmarks.

top_papers.title: In-Context Reward Adaptation for Robust Preference Modeling
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces In-Context Reward Adaptation, a novel framework for robust preference modeling in Large Language Models. It addresses the limitations of static reward models by enabling on-the-fly adaptation to diverse and unseen human preferences. The core method leverages a transformer's in-context learning capabilities to infer reward structures from a few preference demonstrations, offering a more flexible and generalizable approach to aligning LLMs with human values.
top_papers.figure_caption: Inference accuracy (mean ± \( \pm \) std) across different M M

top_papers.title: LLMSurgeon: Diagnosing Data Mixture of Large Language Models
top_papers.abstract: LLMSurgeon introduces Data Mixture Surgery (DMS), a method to infer the domain-level distribution of an LLM's pretraining data solely from its generated text. It frames this as an inverse problem, estimating a calibrated soft confusion matrix to correct domain confusion and recover the original data mixture. This work's contribution is a novel, post-hoc auditing technique for LLM data provenance, addressing the lack of transparency in LLM pretraining.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of Data Mixture Surgery problem and the LLMSurgeon framework for solving it.

top_papers.title: LLUMI: Improving LLM Writing Assistance for Mental Health Support with Online Community Feedback
top_papers.abstract: LLUMI improves LLM-based mental health support by using community feedback from Reddit to fine-tune a generation model and an improvement model. This approach allows for in-house hosting, addressing privacy concerns, and leverages upvote/downvote patterns to create preference data for training, leading to more helpful and empathetic responses.

top_papers.title: Locally Coherent, Globally Incoherent: Bounding Compositional Incoherence in Multi-Component LLM Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a method to quantify and correct "compositional incoherence" in multi-component LLM agents, where individual components are logically sound but their combined output violates probability rules. They formalize this as a "compositional residual" ($\epsilon^*$) and propose a projection method to deterministically repair the composition, ensuring probabilistic consistency. Their contribution lies in providing a runtime computable metric for this failure and a practical solution for achieving global coherence.

top_papers.title: Loong: A Human-Like Long Document Translation Agent with Observe-and-Act Adaptive Context Selection
top_papers.abstract: Loong tackles long document translation by employing a novel 3E memory module (Essence, Exemplar, Entity) to store relevant historical context. Instead of processing all past information, it adaptively selects the most useful context through deep reasoning and reinforcement learning, mimicking human translation strategies. This approach significantly improves translation quality by overcoming context window limitations and reducing redundant information.
top_papers.figure_caption: Cumulative average sCOMET and LLM-as-a-Judge scores of Loong and baseline methods on ultra-long document translation (Chinese ⇒ \( \Rightarrow \) Portuguese). While standard chunking methods (Sentence, Segment), full-history variants (Doc2Doc, Wang et al. , 2023a ) , and unfiltered memory agents ( DelTA , Wang et al. , 2025c ) exhibit continuous degradation or even failure due to context length limits, Loong successfully distinguishes useful information from retrieved memory to sustain stable, high-quality translations. See § 4.4 for more experimental details.

top_papers.title: Same Evidence, Different Answers: Canonical-Context On-Policy Distillation for Multi-Turn Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the issue of multi-turn language models producing different answers to the same information presented gradually versus in a single prompt. Their core method, Canonical-Context On-Policy Distillation (CCOPD), trains a student model to mimic a teacher model's behavior on a complete prompt, even when the student receives information incrementally. This distillation technique aims to prevent self-anchored drift and improve consistency in multi-turn reasoning.
top_papers.figure_caption: Part 1 : Task-equivalent Full , Concat , and Raw-Sharded presentations. Part 2 : Reduced self-anchored drift and improved canonical-context consistency.

top_papers.title: Direct Product Flow Matching: Decoupling Radial and Angular Dynamics for Few-Shot Adaptation
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Direct Product Flow Matching (DPFM) to improve few-shot adaptation in vision-language models by addressing geometric limitations in existing flow matching methods. DPFM decouples the radial and angular dynamics of cross-modal features using a polar decomposition perspective, resolving issues like angular distortion and radial dynamics neglect. This novel approach leads to more effective and stable adaptation by treating radial and angular components independently.
top_papers.figure_caption: (a). Single-step parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) mostly performs cross-modal alignment in a single-step manner. (b). Multi-step flow matching (FM) methods model continuous and multi-step alignment dynamics. During the training stage, (c). FMA undergoes a non-uniform angular speed induced by radial–angular coupling. However, (d). DP-FM follows a constant-speed angular geodesic due to decoupled radial and angular dynamics.

top_papers.title: Position: Mechanistic Interpretability Must Disclose Identification Assumptions for Causal Claims
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that mechanistic interpretability research, which frequently employs causal language, must explicitly state its identification assumptions for causal claims to be valid. The authors audited existing work, finding a pervasive lack of dedicated sections detailing these assumptions, with validation metrics often incorrectly substituted for actual causal identification. The core contribution is a proposed disclosure norm requiring researchers to name their identification strategy, enumerate assumptions, and explain the implications if those assumptions fail.
top_papers.figure_caption: Validation metrics and identification assumptions are not interchangeable. Validation metrics report what the data show under assumed conditions; identification assumptions are the conditions under which a causal claim follows. Substitution—reporting the metric in place of the assumption—leaves the causal claim unidentified. Audit ( n = 10 n=10 + n = 30 n=30 sensitivity): 0 / 30 0/30 papers contain a dedicated identification-assumptions section under any rule or coder.

top_papers.title: Interpreting Reinforcement Learning Agents with Susceptibilities
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces "susceptibilities" as a novel interpretability technique generalized from neural networks to deep reinforcement learning, focusing on the regret's response to loss perturbations. The core contribution is demonstrating that susceptibilities reveal internal developmental features within the model's parameter space that are invisible when only analyzing the learned policy. This method is validated using activation steering and proposed for extension to RLHF post-training.
top_papers.figure_caption: Training dynamics for a model trained with α = 0.6 \( \alpha \)=0.6 . Top row: Cheese-in-corner environment; an RL agent (mouse) navigates to cheese (+1 reward). Initial states are colored by mouse position relative to the cheese (two rightmost panels); in a fraction 1 − α 1-\( \alpha \) of environments cheese is in the top-left corner (top right panel), so all initial mouse positions are red/orange/yellow. Middle and lower row: Middle left shows the LLC and regret curves; the model passes through 3 phases, visible as LLC spikes and regret drops. Red dots mark the checkpoints whose susceptibility scatter plots are shown, projecting susceptibilities to 2D (average convolution-block vs. average fully-connected-block susceptibility).
