Red/orange/yellow states dominate phases 1–2, with other directions concentrated near the origin. Lower left: transition to phase 3, where other directions (notably green) gain non-zero susceptibilities, before two clusters emerge early in phase 3 (lower middle). Late in phase 3, the clusters merge and the overall susceptibility scale declines. Each susceptibility panel reports the variance explained by PC1 and PC1’s cosine similarity to each weight restriction direction.

top_papers.title: When Diffusion Model Can Ignore Dimension: An Entropy-Based Theory
top_papers.abstract: This paper develops an entropy-based theory to explain the high-dimensional efficiency of diffusion model samplers. The core method proves that the discretization error for Gaussian mixture targets is controlled by the **Shannon entropy of the latent mixture components**, not the ambient dimension. This contribution shows that step complexity scales linearly with latent entropy, offering a theoretical justification for why diffusion models perform well even in high dimensions with few steps.

top_papers.title: Shepherd: A Runtime Substrate Empowering Meta-Agents with a Formalized Execution Trace
top_papers.abstract: Shepherd introduces a functional programming model that formalizes meta-agent operations as functions, with core logic verified in Lean. Its key contribution is an efficient, Git-like execution trace that records all agent interactions, allowing past states to be forked and replayed significantly faster than Docker. This infrastructure enables powerful applications like runtime intervention, counterfactual meta-optimization, and efficient reinforcement learning training.
top_papers.figure_caption: Shepherd meta-agents. Top: A supervisor meta-agent manages code repair agents. Bottom: Results from three meta-agents: (A) live supervision; (B) meta-optimization; (C) Tree GRPO

top_papers.title: SLIM: Sparse Latent Steering for Interpretable and Property-Directed LLM-Based Molecular Editing
top_papers.abstract: SLIM introduces a plug-and-play framework that uses a Sparse Autoencoder with importance gates to decompose LLM hidden states into sparse, property-aligned features. This allows for explicit steering in the latent space, precisely activating property-relevant dimensions to improve molecular editing success rates without altering the underlying LLM parameters. The method consistently improves editing performance across various models and properties while also enabling interpretable analysis of the editing process.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the SLIM framework. Stage 1 : Ridge probes scan all layers to identify the optimal intervention point l ∗ l^{*} . Stage 2 : A task-oriented SAE is trained at layer l ∗ l^{*} with four objectives: (A) sparse reconstruction, (B) supervised property prediction via per-property Importance Gates, (C) contrastive alignment of importance-gated sparse codes, and (D) gradient alignment to ensure the SAE basis faithfully represents causal steering directions. Stage 3 : At inference time, a sparse steering vector is added to the residual stream at layer l ∗ l^{*} , directing the model toward improved molecular properties without modifying model parameters.

top_papers.title: (How) Do Large Language Models Understand High-Level Message Sequence Charts?
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether Large Language Models (LLMs) truly understand the formal semantics of High-Level Message Sequence Charts (HMSCs), a crucial visual modeling language for software architecture. The researchers tested three LLMs on 129 semantic tasks, ranging from basic querying to complex operations like abstraction and composition, to assess their consistency with HMSC semantics. The core contribution is to empirically evaluate LLMs' capabilities in handling formal, visual specifications, highlighting potential limitations in their current "understanding" of such artifacts.
top_papers.figure_caption: An MSC labelled “example1”, with four instances, labelled “i1” to “i4”, five messages labelled “m0” to “m4”, and an internal action labelled “a”.

top_papers.title: Children's English Reading Story Generation via Supervised Fine-Tuning of Compact LLMs with Controllable Difficulty and Safety
top_papers.abstract: This paper fine-tunes compact LLMs (8B parameters) using expert-designed children's reading curricula and existing generated stories. The core method focuses on controllable difficulty and safety, allowing educators to target specific reading levels. The key contribution is demonstrating that these smaller, more affordable LLMs can generate children's English reading stories that outperform larger models on difficulty metrics, making them more practical for educational use.
top_papers.figure_caption: System architecture and experimental workflow for generating children’s English reading stories via supervised fine-tuning of compact LLMs.

top_papers.title: EVA-Bench: A New End-to-end Framework for Evaluating Voice Agents
top_papers.abstract: EVA-Bench is a novel end-to-end framework for evaluating voice agents. It addresses the limitations of existing benchmarks by generating realistic, dynamic bot-to-bot audio conversations and introducing two comprehensive metrics: EVA-A for task completion and speech fidelity, and EVA-X for conversational experience and timing. This framework provides a more holistic and robust evaluation of voice agent performance.
top_papers.figure_caption: EVA-Bench framework overview. The simulation orchestrates parallel per-scenario bot-to-bot audio sessions over WebSocket in which the User Simulator — configured with a scenario-specific goal, persona, and conversational TTS voice — interacts with the Voice Agent under test. The Tool Executor handles all agent tool calls deterministically. Completed conversations pass through Simulator Validation that trigger automatic regeneration on failure before entering the Quality Measurements phase, which produces EVA-A and EVA-X pass@1, pass@k, and pass^k scores in addition to Diagnostic metrics.

top_papers.title: Harnessing Agentic Evolution
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces AEvo, a meta-editing framework for agentic evolution. AEvo treats the evolution process as an interactive environment, using accumulated evidence as its state. A meta-agent then modifies the evolution procedure itself, rather than directly generating new candidates, to improve long-horizon search and prevent drift.
top_papers.figure_caption: Harnessing agentic evolution as an interactive environment. (a) Procedure-based evolution runs a fixed loop for selection, optimization, evaluation, and update. (b) Agent-based evolution lets a general-purpose agent manage search through feedback, tools, skills, and code actions. (c) AEvo treats the evolution process as an interactive environment. The accumulated evolution context becomes process-level state, while a meta-agent edits the underlying procedure or agent operating context that controls future evolution.

top_papers.title: MILM: Large Language Models for Multimodal Irregular Time Series with Informative Sampling
top_papers.abstract: MILM represents multimodal irregular time series (MITS) as XML triplets to leverage Large Language Models (LLMs). Its core method involves a two-stage fine-tuning process: first, predicting from sampling patterns alone, then jointly modeling patterns and observed values. This approach contributes by effectively capturing predictive signals from both the timing and content of irregular, asynchronous multimodal data, particularly in healthcare.

top_papers.title: Confirming Correct, Missing the Rest: LLM Tutoring Agents Struggle Where Feedback Matters Most
top_papers.abstract: This paper evaluates LLM tutoring agents' ability to distinguish between correct, suboptimal, and incorrect student reasoning in propositional logic. The core method involves a benchmark of seven LLM feedback agents tested against knowledge-graph-derived ground truth. The key contribution is demonstrating that LLMs excel at identifying optimal steps but systematically fail to accurately diagnose suboptimal or incorrect reasoning, precisely where adaptive tutoring is most crucial.
top_papers.figure_caption: Optimal and valid-alternative solutions (blue nodes represent abbreviated inference rule names, explained in Table 4 )

top_papers.title: paper.json: A Coordination Convention for LLM-Agent-Actionable Papers
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces `paper.json`, a companion JSON file to academic PDFs, designed to improve LLM agent comprehension and actionability. Its core method involves adding structured metadata like stable claim IDs and explicit "does-not-claim" lists to address LLM failures in understanding paper scope and reproducibility. The main contribution is a lightweight, easily implementable convention that enables LLM agents to more reliably extract and act upon information within academic papers.

top_papers.title: Prospective multi-pathogen disease forecasting using autonomous LLM-guided tree search
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces an autonomous system that uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to guide a tree search for generating and optimizing infectious disease forecasting models. The LLM-guided approach allows for rapid, scalable development of diverse models, outperforming human-curated ensembles and effectively handling data-scarce situations. This significantly advances prospective disease forecasting by automating the complex and time-consuming model curation process.

top_papers.title: Learn Where Outcomes Diverge: Efficient VLA RL via Probabilistic Chunk Masking
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes Probabilistic Chunk Masking to improve the efficiency of Vision-Language-Action Reinforcement Learning (VLA RL). The core method identifies and masks "non-divergent" trajectory segments that offer little learning signal, focusing computational resources on crucial phases where successful and failed outcomes diverge. This significantly reduces gradient computation costs, leading to faster and more efficient policy updates.
top_papers.figure_caption: Left: per-phase success–failure action variance ( C c C_{c} ) concentrates on decision-critical phases. Right: gradient computation dominates wall-clock training cost in simulator-based GRPO with 10 rollouts per prompt. Remaining ∼ \( \sim \) 1% is logging, evaluation, and inter-step overhead.

top_papers.title: A Methodology for Selecting and Composing Runtime Architecture Patterns for Production LLM Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the "stochastic-deterministic boundary" (SDB) as a core architectural primitive for production LLM agents, defining a four-part contract for integrating LLM outputs into system actions. It then proposes six runtime patterns, organized around Coordination, State, and Control, to compose this SDB for various agent types, adapting distributed systems concepts to the stochastic nature of LLMs. The contribution lies in providing a structured methodology and reusable patterns for building robust LLM agent runtimes.

top_papers.title: CopT: Contrastive On-Policy Thinking with Continuous Spaces for General and Agentic Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: CopT reverses the traditional Chain-of-Thought approach by first generating a draft answer and then performing "on-policy thinking" to reflect and correct it. It uses continuous embeddings as contrastive verifiers to assess the trustworthiness of the draft answer by comparing token support under discrete and continuous inputs. This method aims to improve reasoning efficiency and accuracy by allowing for earlier access to plausible answers and more targeted refinement.
top_papers.figure_caption: (a) Conceptual comparison between CoT thinking and CopT on-policy thinking. (b) CopT contrasts the output distributions under discrete and continuous inputs. (c) CopT improves peak accuracy, marked by ∗ , across mathematics, coding, and agentic reasoning tasks and nearly halves token usage at matched accuracy.

top_papers.title: Less Back-and-Forth: A Comparative Study of Structured Prompting
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how structured prompts affect Large Language Model (LLM) performance. It compares raw prompts, checklist-improved prompts, and clarifying-question prompts across various tasks and LLM systems. The core contribution is demonstrating that checklist-improved prompts significantly enhance response quality and efficiency, achieving the highest scores and best quality-effort tradeoff.
top_papers.figure_caption: Study design overview. For each task, a raw prompt is evaluated alongside a checklist-improved prompt and a clarifying-question prompt. Each prompt condition is tested across multiple LLMs, and the resulting outputs are scored using the same rubric.

top_papers.title: Probing Embodied LLMs: When Higher Observation Fidelity Hurts Problem Solving
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how observation fidelity impacts embodied Large Language Models (LLMs) in robotic tasks. The core method involves empirically testing LLM agents on a mechanical puzzle with varying levels of visual and symbolic information. The surprising contribution is that LLMs perform *better* with less precise, raw RGB input than with perfect ground-truth observations, suggesting that moderate noise can actually enhance their problem-solving abilities by making them more robust to uncertainty.
top_papers.figure_caption: Our robotic system manipulating the Lockbox. Our Lockbox comprises two prismatic joints (sliding bars in the middle) and two revolute joints. The Lockbox is unlocked when the leftmost revolute joint, which we refer to as the target joint, is pulled. The robot employs a soft-hand end effector for manipulating the joints, an RGB-D camera for acquiring visual data, and a force-torque sensor for assessing the joint movability and guiding their manipulation.

top_papers.title: What Do Evolutionary Coding Agents Evolve?
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces EvoTrace, a dataset of evolutionary coding traces, to address the question of what these systems actually evolve. Their core method, EvoReplay, analyzes these traces to distinguish between genuine algorithmic innovation and other mechanisms like re-tuning or overfitting. The contribution is a novel methodology and dataset for understanding the internal workings of evolutionary coding agents.
top_papers.figure_caption: A taxonomy of edits performed by evolutionary coding agents. Each panel shows a representative parent–child diff (added lines in green, deleted lines in red) drawn from EvoTrace runs and labeled with one of nine recurring categories: Bug fix , External dependency , Architectural change , Composition , Local refinement , Pruning , Refactor , Efficiency , and Hyperparameter tuning . The categories range from minimal numeric edits (a single literal change) to structural rewrites (replacing a 14-gon with two concentric heptagons), and they form the basis of the LLM-as-judge edit annotation used throughout the paper. Edits are typically multi-label; we examine prevalence and per-edit utility in § 5.1 .

top_papers.title: MixRea: Benchmarking Explicit-Implicit Reasoning in Large Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces "explicit-implicit reasoning" to benchmark LLMs' ability to attend to subtle cues, inspired by human inattentional blindness. They created the MixRea benchmark and found even top LLMs struggle with this, exhibiting widespread inattentional blindness. To address this, they propose Potential Relation Completion Prompting (PRCP), a method that improves reasoning by recovering overlooked causal relations.
top_papers.figure_caption: An explicit-implicit reasoning example from our MixRea benchmark. When reasoning about explicitly stated information in the question, LLMs must leverage distinctions among events presented in the options to identify and infer relevant implicit information from the story context. They then integrate these reasoning results to derive the optimal event set.

top_papers.title: Rewarding Beliefs, Not Actions: Consistency-Guided Credit Assignment for Long-Horizon Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ReBel, a reinforcement learning algorithm for long-horizon tasks where agents learn from verifiable rewards. ReBel addresses challenges in partially observable environments by explicitly modeling and updating agent beliefs. Its core contribution is a belief-consistency supervision mechanism that generates dense self-supervised signals from belief discrepancies, leading to more robust credit assignment without external annotations.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of ReBel. ReBel learns belief-aware policies for partially observable long-horizon tasks by making latent belief explicit and decomposing policy generation into belief, think, and action. It turns sparse terminal rewards into step-wise belief consistency feedback and performs belief-anchor grouping to support stable step-level advantage estimation.

top_papers.title: WikiVQABench: A Knowledge-Grounded Visual Question Answering Benchmark from Wikipedia and Wikidata
top_papers.abstract: WikiVQABench is a new benchmark for knowledge-grounded Visual Question Answering. Its core method involves using LLMs to generate candidate VQA questions from Wikipedia images and Wikidata, which are then human-curated to ensure they require external knowledge beyond visual perception. The contribution is a substantial dataset designed to evaluate models' ability to integrate visual information with real-world knowledge.
top_papers.figure_caption: Example from WikiVQABench illustrating a knowledge-grounded multiple-choice VQA instance. The image depicts a spider whose taxonomic classification cannot be determined from visual appearance alone. Correctly answering the question requires external biological knowledge linking visual cues to entity-level taxonomy (e.g., family or genus), demonstrating the benchmark’s emphasis on required knowledge beyond surface-level perception.

top_papers.title: DelTA: Discriminative Token Credit Assignment for Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes DelTA, a method to improve reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) for large language models. It frames RLVR updates as a linear discriminator that guides token probability changes. DelTA's core contribution is a novel centroid construction that mitigates the dominance of common patterns, allowing the model to better identify and learn from discriminative token-level signals for improved reasoning.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of DelTA. DelTA estimates token coefficients from the contrast between positive- and negative-advantage token-gradient aggregates, and uses the coefficients to reweight the sequence-level RLVR objective.

top_papers.title: Memorisation, convergence and generalisation in generative models
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether generative models truly learn data distributions or just memorize training examples. The authors show that diffusion models trained on separate data subsets converge to similar distributions when the dataset is large enough. They then analytically characterize this transition from memorization to generalization in linear models, finding convergence emerges continuously when sample size scales linearly with input dimension, and surprisingly, this convergence is independent of recovering key data features.
top_papers.figure_caption: Memorisation, convergence, and latent recovery in generative models. Left : We illustrate three regimes in generative models by sketching the principal components (top) and the covariance spectra (bottom) of the true data distribution (blue) and a distribution learnt from n n samples (red) in d d dimensions. For very small data sets, n = Θ ​ ( 1 ) n=\( \Theta \)(1) , the model does not learn a smooth approximation of the data distribution, but memorises the training set. As the number of training samples becomes comparable to the input dimension, n ≍ d n\( \asymp \) d , the spectra continuously converge and independently trained models produce similar outputs given the same latents, as described by [ undef ] . However, convergence of outputs does not imply recovery of the principal latent, which corresponds to the outlier in the true spectrum. The principal latent is learnt independently from the rest of the spectrum in a sharp transition. Right : We develop this picture mathematically by developing an asymptotically exact theory that describes the evolution of three order parameters for memorisation ( m m ), convergence ( q q ), and latent recovery ( Q Q ) in linear generative models. The two lines for Q Q correspond to high and low signal-to-noise ratio \( \beta \) of the principal latent (dark and light green, respectively).

top_papers.title: Preference-aware Influence-function-based Data Selection Method for Efficient Fine-Tuning
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces PRISM, a novel data selection method for efficient LLM fine-tuning. PRISM's core method is to weight target examples based on the current model's preference, creating a more nuanced representation of the desired behavior. This allows PRISM to prioritize candidate training samples that are most aligned with this preference-aware target, leading to more effective use of limited training budgets.
top_papers.figure_caption: Motivation of PRISM. Uniform aggregation treats all target examples equally, whereas PRISM emphasizes target examples closer to the current model and focuses the selection budget on more actionable training samples.

top_papers.title: What Twelve LLM Agent Benchmark Papers Disclose About Themselves: A Pilot Audit and an Open Scoring Schema
top_papers.abstract: This paper audits twelve LLM agent benchmark papers to assess the completeness of their evaluation reporting. The authors developed a five-field schema (benchmark identity, harness, inference settings, cost, failure breakdown) to systematically record what each paper discloses about its evaluation methodology. Their contribution is an open scoring schema and a pilot audit, highlighting the lack of transparency in current benchmark reporting and its impact on reproducibility.

top_papers.title: "I didn't Make the Micro Decisions": Measuring, Inducing, and Exposing Goal-Level AI Contributions in Collaboration
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces CoTrace, a framework to measure and attribute AI's contributions to collaborative goal formation, focusing on the process rather than just the final output. CoTrace decomposes goals into requirements and traces direct and indirect influences across dialogue turns. The study reveals that while LLMs contribute less to overall goal shaping, they are significant in generating concrete requirements and exert indirect influence, with interaction design impacting their behavior.
top_papers.figure_caption: Illustrative overview of CoTrace and its benefits. CoTrace analyzes human and LLM contributions at the goal level by tracing requirement lifecycles, including direct contributions (who explicitly creates a requirement) and indirect contributions (who influences another party to introduce it). It supports measuring goal-shaping behavior, provides a signal for inducing it, and supports user awareness by exposing contribution dynamics.

top_papers.title: AMEL: Accumulated Message Effects on LLM Judgments
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates the "accumulated message effect" (AMEL), demonstrating that the sentiment of prior conversation history biases Large Language Models' (LLMs) subsequent judgments. The core method involves presenting identical test items to LLMs either in isolation or after conversations saturated with positive or negative evaluations. The key contribution is the finding that LLMs shift their judgments towards the prevailing polarity of the conversation, particularly when uncertain, and this bias is independent of context length, with a notable negativity asymmetry.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of AMEL. (a) Items where the model is uncertain at baseline absorb the most bias ( d = − 0.34 d=-0.34 ); confident-baseline items absorb less ( d = − 0.15 d=-0.15 ). (b) Negative context biases models more than positive context (paired per-item ratio 1.62 × 1.62\( \times \) , p < 10 − 39 p<10^{-39} ); marginal means yield ≈ 2 × \( \approx \) 2\( \times \) (Section 4.5 ). Even balanced history shifts models toward “no.” (c) Bias saturates immediately; 5 turns produce the same effect as 50.

top_papers.title: DeltaBox: Scaling Stateful AI Agents with Millisecond-Level Sandbox Checkpoint/Rollback
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces DeltaBox, a system that enables rapid checkpointing and rollback for stateful AI agents. Its core method is to only duplicate changes between consecutive sandbox states, rather than the entire state, significantly reducing latency. This contribution allows for high-frequency state exploration, crucial for advanced AI agent functionalities like deep search and reinforcement learning.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . Pass rate on SWE-bench Verified. (a) Linear ReAct vs. MCTS across three coding models. (b) Base vs. RL-trained across three open-weight model families.

top_papers.title: LCGuard: Latent Communication Guard for Safe KV Sharing in Multi-Agent Systems
top_papers.abstract: LCGuard introduces a framework to secure latent communication in multi-agent LLM systems that use KV caches. It works by learning representation-level transformations on shared KV caches before they are transmitted between agents, preventing sensitive information from leaking through this opaque channel. This ensures safer coordination and information sharing in complex multi-agent tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: Multi-agent communication topologies: sequential, hierarchical, and graph-based. Edges carry KV cache latent artifacts m i ​ j m_{ij} .

top_papers.title: MOSS: Self-Evolution through Source-Level Rewriting in Autonomous Agent Systems
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces MOSS, a system that enables autonomous agents to evolve by rewriting their own source code. Unlike previous methods that only modify text-based configurations, MOSS directly alters the agent's underlying program, allowing it to fix structural failures and adapt more fundamentally. This source-level rewriting offers a Turing-complete and deterministic approach to agent evolution, overcoming limitations of text-based adaptation.

top_papers.title: Understanding Data Temporality Impact on Large Language Models Pre-training
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how data ordering during LLM pre-training affects temporal knowledge. The authors introduce a benchmark of temporally grounded questions and show that pre-training LLMs on temporally ordered data, rather than shuffled data, leads to models with more accurate and up-to-date factual knowledge without sacrificing general language understanding.
top_papers.figure_caption: Yearly temporal knowledge with Kairos. Relative gains in F1 score on KairosQA between the 2020–2021 and 2023–2024 periods for our sequentially pre-trained model versus other open-source base models (ordered by their release date with the most recent at the right). These results highlight that even for recently released open-source base models, shuffled pre-training leads to a temporal delay in knowledge; performance decays when querying recent facts, even those preceding the training cut-off. Conversely, sequential pre-training represents a significant step toward developing more up-to-date models.

top_papers.title: SeqLoRA: Bilevel Orthogonal Adaptation for Continual Multi-Concept Generation
top_papers.abstract: SeqLoRA addresses the challenge of generating multiple custom concepts with text-to-image models by using a bilevel optimization approach to sequentially fine-tune LoRA factors. This method jointly optimizes these factors, preventing interference between concepts and improving identity preservation. Its core contribution lies in a constrained continual learning framework that offers theoretical guarantees against catastrophic forgetting and demonstrates superior performance in multi-concept generation.
top_papers.figure_caption: Qualitative comparison of multi-concept image generation across different methods for 32 concepts. We show the original concept images (Input) and 4 generated samples (seeds) for all methods.

top_papers.title: ChronoMedKG: A Temporally-Grounded Biomedical Knowledge Graph and Benchmark for Clinical Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ChronoMedKG, a novel biomedical knowledge graph that explicitly incorporates temporal information for clinical findings and disease associations. Unlike static KGs, ChronoMedKG grounds each relation with temporal context (e.g., age of onset, progression stage) and evidence, enabling more accurate longitudinal clinical reasoning. Its core contribution is a temporally-aware KG and a benchmark that addresses the limitations of existing static KGs for time-sensitive medical applications.
top_papers.figure_caption: ChronoMedKG: from what is associated to when it emerges. (A) Across 17,080 PrimeKG diseases, zero edges carry temporal annotations. (B) 4-agent pipeline ingests ∼ \( \sim \) 2.5M public documents and produces literature-grounded temporal triples with PMID provenance, multi-LLM consensus, and six-signal credibility scoring. (C) Downstream outputs unique to ChronoMedKG. (D) Top-line numbers. Constructed de novo from public literature; no patient-level records.

top_papers.title: Beyond Binary Edits Robust Multimodal Knowledge Editing with Adversarial Subspace Alignment
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Latent Adversarial Robustification (LAR) to improve multimodal knowledge editing. LAR generates adversarial variations of input data in the latent space to expose and strengthen fragile semantic regions, enabling more generalizable knowledge updates that extend beyond specific examples. The core contribution is a method that enhances the robustness and generality of multimodal knowledge editing by explicitly addressing semantic equivalence and adversarial perturbations.
top_papers.figure_caption: The overall framework of ASAM, which consists of two key modules. ❶ LAR. Given multimodal inputs, LAR perturbs input embeddings along LLM-guided gradients to generate semantically consistent rephrases. ❷ RCSL. Using these rephrases, RCSL applies SVD-based subspace learning to align editing-layer outputs, enforcing semantic consistency across variants.

top_papers.title: CVSearch: Empowering Multimodal LLMs with Cognitive Visual Search for High-Resolution Image Perception
top_papers.abstract: CVSearch tackles the challenge of high-resolution image perception in multimodal LLMs by dynamically adapting its search strategy. It first attempts an efficient expert-assisted search and, if that fails, employs a novel semantic-aware scanning method that intelligently partitions images into meaningful regions, avoiding fragmentation and improving efficiency. This training-free approach enhances coverage without computational redundancy.

top_papers.title: From Raw Experience to Skill Consumption: A Systematic Study of Model-Generated Agent Skills
top_papers.abstract: This paper systematically studies the full lifecycle of model-generated agent skills, from experience generation to skill extraction and consumption. It introduces a utility-grounded evaluation framework to assess the effectiveness of these skills across diverse domains. The core contribution is a comprehensive analysis revealing that while model-generated skills are generally beneficial, their performance is not always consistent and depends on various factors.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of our study design. We evaluate the full trajectory-to-skill lifecycle across three stages: experience generation, skill extraction, and skill consumption.

top_papers.title: Human Decision-Making with Persuasive and Narrative LLM Explanations
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how persuasive narrative explanations from LLMs affect human decision-making in classification tasks. Through a large-scale experiment, the researchers found that the persuasiveness of LLM explanations did not significantly improve decision accuracy compared to a simple AI prediction. However, narratives did increase reliance on the AI's output.
top_papers.figure_caption: Left: Average participant accuracy across the two dataset conditions and four explanation conditions. Center: Average participant reliance rate across the two dataset conditions and four explanation conditions. Right: Predicted effects (level 2/overall results of multilevel model) of confidence ratings and dataset upon accuracy. Steeper, positively-sloped lines indicate better confidence calibration. Across all plots, error bars and shaded areas represent 95% confidence intervals.

top_papers.title: It's the humans, not the data: Geopolitical bias in LLMs originates in post-training, amplified by the language of the prompt
top_papers.abstract: This paper demonstrates that geopolitical bias in LLMs primarily arises during the post-training (fine-tuning) phase, not from the initial training data. The researchers found that models often develop a bias favoring the country of their developer after fine-tuning, and this bias is amplified by the language of the prompt.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview, seven families. (A) Per-country preference base → \( \to \) post-trained; for the six non-GLM bases, cross-country spread \( \sigma \) grows post-training (Qwen 3.9 → 30.3 3.9\( \to \) 30.3 pp). (B) Post-training \( \Delta \) in China-favourability (EN, coherent subset). 3/3 Western labs shift anti-China; 3/4 Chinese labs shift pro-China; Yi shifts anti-China after prefill correction. GLM is shown with its (atypical) base preserved for completeness; see § Bias Is Created by Post-Training, Not Pretraining . The legend’s low-compliance encoding is described in § What MCQ Compliance Tells Us About Validity . (C) ZH − - EN shift on post-trained models: 5/7 descriptively pro-China but population-level claim is not statistically separable from the base trend (§ Linguistic Identity Modulates the Post-Training Bias ).

top_papers.title: Leveraging Foundation Models for Causal Generative Modeling
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces FM-CGM, a modular framework that leverages pretrained foundation models for visual causal reasoning. Its core method involves a three-component pipeline (concept extractor, manipulator, and generator) that enables zero-shot causal discovery, intervention, and counterfactual generation. The key contribution is the development of Causal Semantic Guidance (CSG), a mechanism that ensures semantic interventions propagate correctly through the causal graph.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . An overview of Foundation Model Powered Causal Generative Model (FM-CGM) consisting of a concept extractor, concept manipulator, and counterfactual generator enabled by foundation models

top_papers.title: LLMs as Noisy Channels: A Shannon Perspective on Model Capacity and Scaling Laws
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes the Shannon Scaling Law, modeling LLM training as information transmission over a noisy channel. By relating model parameters to bandwidth and training data to signal power, it explains non-monotonic performance changes like overtraining and quantization degradation. The core contribution is a theoretical framework that predicts a fundamental Shannon capacity for LLMs, where insufficient signal-to-noise ratio leads to performance deterioration.
top_papers.figure_caption: Loss landscapes between Pretraining and downstream SFT. While pretraining exhibits monotonic improvement, SFT reveals a loss basin, indicating that scaling either model size or token count beyond a critical threshold leads to performance degradation.

top_papers.title: MemAudit: Post-hoc Auditing of Poisoned Agent Memory via Causal Attribution and Structural Anomaly Detection
top_papers.abstract: MemAudit is a post-hoc framework designed to identify and audit malicious memories injected into LLM agents. It uses causal attribution to measure how each memory influences harmful outputs and structural anomaly detection to pinpoint inconsistent memory entries. This allows for the identification of poisoned memories after an attack has occurred, addressing a gap in existing online defense mechanisms.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of MemAudit. Given a harmful event e = ( q ∗ , y ∗ , R ∗ ) e=(q^{*},y^{*},R^{*}) , the framework performs post-hoc auditing over the memory store. It combines two complementary signals: CMIS, which measures the causal contribution of retrieved memories through counterfactual replay, and MCG, which identifies structurally anomalous memories in the global memory graph. The two signals are fused into a detoxification score for ranking suspicious memories. After removing the top-ranked memories, the agent becomes safer while preserving useful memory.

top_papers.title: OnePred: Next-Query Prediction via Recursive Intent Memory in Multi-Turn Conversations
top_papers.abstract: OnePred addresses the limitation of reactive LLM conversational systems by proposing a novel method for next-query prediction. Its core method, "recursive intent memory," efficiently tracks the user's evolving intent across a conversation without needing to process the full dialogue history. This allows for proactive interaction by anticipating the user's next query with a constant per-turn computational cost, overcoming the efficiency-quality trade-off of existing approaches.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the NQP-Bench construction pipeline. The process integrates heuristic filtering, a two-stage LLM cascade for rigorous predictability screening, and a retroactive truncation mining strategy to salvage high-quality conversation prefixes from the DROP pool.

top_papers.title: PGT: Procedurally Generated Tasks for improving visual grounding in MLLMs
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Procedurally Generated Tasks (PGT), a data-driven framework that enhances visual grounding in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). PGT overlays geometric primitives on images to create dense supervision, disentangling visual understanding from semantic knowledge. This method significantly improves performance on fine-grained tasks and serves as a diagnostic tool for identifying perception failures.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of PGT. Top : The construction of our procedurally generated data to augment instruction tuning training datasets. Abstract geometric primitives are overlaid to training data, when available. Bottom : (Left) Examples of failure modes in fine-grained relational and spatial understanding of state-of-the-art MLLMs. In the first example the model can rely on the fact that a bowl is usually on a table and in the second example it can rely on the shortcut where object higher up as usually further from the camera (Right) PGT performance boosts in relational, quantitative, and 3D/depth understanding over the baseline when using different instruction tuning dataset sizes.

top_papers.title: SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills
top_papers.abstract: SkillOpt treats agent skills as trainable external states, optimizing them with the same rigor as deep learning weights. Its core method involves a separate optimizer model that makes bounded edits to a skill document, accepting only those that strictly improve performance. This approach offers the first systematic, controllable text-space optimization for agent skills, leading to reliable improvement without increasing inference cost.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of SkillOpt . The target model executes tasks with a current skill, an additional frontier optimizer model converts trajectories into bounded add/delete/replace skill edits, and a held-out gate accepts only edits that improve validation performance. Accepted edits are exported as a reusable skill artifact, while rejected edits become negative feedback for later updates.

top_papers.title: Approaching I/O-optimality for Approximate Attention
top_papers.abstract: This paper presents an I/O-efficient algorithm for computing attention in large language models, significantly reducing data transfers compared to existing methods. By adapting approximate attention techniques, their approach achieves an almost-linear I/O cost with respect to the input size, approaching theoretical optimality.

top_papers.title: Learning Kernel-Based MDPs from Episodic Preferential Feedback
top_papers.abstract: This paper develops a theoretical framework for learning Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with kernel-based reward and transition functions using only episodic preferential feedback. The core method involves estimating values and confidence sets from binary preference labels between policy trajectories, modeled by a Bradley-Terry-Luce link. The key contribution is proving sublinear regret bounds, demonstrating that this preference-based approach can effectively learn optimal policies from human-like feedback.

top_papers.title: SeedER: Seed-and-Expand Retrieval from Knowledge Graphs
top_papers.abstract: SeedER tackles knowledge graph retrieval by first identifying a small set of relevant "seed" nodes using efficient methods. It then iteratively expands this set using a learned policy that prioritizes low-cost, graph-aware steps, effectively decomposing complex queries into manageable local decisions. This approach efficiently discovers query-relevant information while controlling computational expense, overcoming limitations of purely dense retrieval for multi-hop queries.

top_papers.title: Strong Teacher Not Needed? On Distillation in LLM Pretraining
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates knowledge distillation in LLM pretraining, challenging the assumption that a stronger teacher is always necessary. They demonstrate that even smaller, undertrained teachers can significantly benefit larger students when distillation loss is properly mixed with language modeling loss. Furthermore, excessively strong teachers can lead to diminishing or even negative distillation gains, and distillation primarily enhances generalization rather than in-domain fitting.

top_papers.title: How Human-Like Are Large Language Models? A Register-Aware Linguistic Evaluation Framework
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel framework for evaluating the linguistic human-likeness of Large Language Models (LLMs). It moves beyond factual correctness to assess if LLM-generated text adheres to context-dependent linguistic patterns observed in human language. The core method uses a statistical test (MMD) to compare feature distributions between human and LLM corpora, contributing a more nuanced understanding of LLM text quality.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the proposed evaluation framework. Given a specific Register r r , we collect a human and LLM-generated corpus. Then we extract a set of 67 linguistic features from the texts. Finally, we calculate the distance between the human sample and the synthetic corpus and resample the distance between human samples as a baseline.

top_papers.title: Calibrating Conservatism for Scalable Oversight
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Calibrated Collective Oversight (CCO), a method for scalable oversight of autonomous AI systems. CCO aggregates diverse overseer scores into a penalty that measures deviation from a conservative baseline, allowing high-utility actions unless overseer concern accumulates. Its key contribution is online calibration using Conformal Decision Theory, guaranteeing undesirable outcomes stay below a user-defined threshold with statistical confidence.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of Calibrated Collective Oversight (CCO). Given a state s s , a primary agent either generates candidate actions { a 1 , a 2 , a 3 , … } \{a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},\( \ldots \)\} or receives a fixed set from the environment, assigning each a utility score U ​ ( s , a ) U(s,a) reflecting its own preferences; a conservative baseline a o a_{o} (e.g., defer or no-op) is always included. These candidates, which may include actions with hidden vulnerabilities or misaligned objectives, are evaluated by a collection of auxiliary overseers { q 1 , … , q n } \{q_{1},\( \ldots \),q_{n}\} , each assessing a different dimension such as scope, safety, or convention adherence. The aggregate penalty Δ ​ ( s , a ) = ∑ i | q i ​ ( s , a ) − q i ​ ( s , a o ) | \( \Delta \)(s,a)=\( \sum_{i} \)|q_{i}(s,a)-q_{i}(s,a_{o})| measures how much each action deviates from the baseline across all oversight signals. CCO selects actions by maximizing U ​ ( s , a ) − λ t ​ Δ ​ ( s , a ) U(s,a)-\( \lambda_{t} \)\( \Delta \)(s,a) , where the conservatism parameter \( \lambda_{t} \) is updated online via a conformal controller: after observing whether the selected action incurred a loss ℓ t \( \ell_{t} \) , the controller adjusts λ t + 1 = λ t + η ​ ( ℓ t − α ) \( \lambda_{t+1} \)=\( \lambda_{t} \)+\( \eta \)(\( \ell_{t} \)-\( \alpha \)) . This feedback loop ensures that realized violation rates converge to the user-specified target \( \alpha \) . In safe situations, CCO relaxes conservatism to permit high-utility actions; in risky situations, it increases conservatism to favor safer alternatives.

top_papers.title: Do Agents Need Semantic Metadata? A Comparative Study in Agentic Data Retrieval
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether autonomous agents still need semantic metadata for data retrieval in the age of LLMs. It compares an agent searching the open web with one using schema.org-annotated datasets, evaluating their performance against FAIR principles using an LLM-as-a-judge approach. The study's contribution lies in its comparative analysis to determine the necessity of semantic metadata for effective agentic data discovery.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparative System Architecture. Similar agent logic is evaluated across unstructured Baseline Agent and Semantic Agent dataset search environments. Both feed a unified, FAIR-aligned evaluation of relevance, accessibility, and utility.

top_papers.title: LiveBrowseComp: Are Search Agents Searching, or Just Verifying What They Already Know?
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether LLM search agents truly search or just verify their internal knowledge. They introduce three diagnostics to study this, revealing "Intrinsic Knowledge Dependence" where agents often rely on pre-existing knowledge over external evidence. To address this, they propose LiveBrowseComp, a new benchmark designed to evaluate agents on evidence-driven discovery rather than memory recall.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of LiveBrowseComp. As models iterate, the knowledge required by a static benchmark is gradually absorbed into their parameters, so the effective difficulty of its questions collapses over time. By being constructed from up-to-date knowledge, LiveBrowseComp can effectively mitigate this erosion.

top_papers.title: Multi-Adapter Representation Interventions via Energy Calibration
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Multi-Adapter Representation Interventions via Energy Calibration (MARI) to improve large language model alignment. MARI addresses limitations of uniform interventions by using specialized adapters that adaptively determine intervention direction and strength per input. An energy-based gating module further refines this by identifying inputs suitable for intervention, thereby preserving general capabilities.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison of intervention results. (1) Alignment Reliability: Static methods (left) rely on a fixed adapter, which leads to unstable performance. In contrast, MARI (right) ensures consistent correctness across diverse inputs. (2) General Capability Preservation: Existing methods intervene indiscriminately, which impairs general capability. MARI overcomes this by employing an energy gate that distinguishes intervention-applicable inputs from benign ones, preserving the model’s general capabilities.

top_papers.title: Towards Reliable Multilingual LLMs-as-a-Judge: An Empirical Study
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how to reliably use Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges for text evaluation in multiple languages, especially for low-resource ones. The core method involves empirically studying different fine-tuning and prompting strategies across English, Spanish, and Basque, considering data availability and model size. The main contribution is identifying key trade-offs, showing that fine-tuned smaller models can be effective with in-domain data, while large models are better for zero-shot multilingual evaluation.
top_papers.figure_caption: Density plot of predictions against gold labels on the in-domain RECON benchmark. We denote configurations as X → Y X\( \rightarrow \) Y , where X X indicates the training-time language setup and Y Y the evaluation-time language setup. lang refers to fully translated instructions ( es , eu , or multi ), and io_lang refers to partially translated instructions, where only the original instructions and model outputs are translated while the rest is kept in English.

top_papers.title: LLM Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning is an Inference Workload
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that Zeroth-Order (ZO) fine-tuning for LLMs is primarily an inference workload, not a traditional training one. By executing the repeated scoring phase through an inference-optimized runtime (vLLM), they achieve significant speedups (8.13x on OPT-13B SST-2) while maintaining high accuracy, demonstrating a more efficient approach to ZO fine-tuning.
top_papers.figure_caption: OPT-13B SST-2 evaluation versus training time. The vLLM ν = 50 \( \nu \)=50 run is shown alongside the official LoZO full and LoRA-only baselines; it finishes in 30.7 30.7 minutes versus 249.2 249.2 minutes for the official LoZO LoRA-only baseline, an 8.13 × 8.13\( \times \) matched-setting speedup.

top_papers.title: Understanding Generalization and Forgetting in In-Context Continual Learning
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the first theoretical framework to understand how Large Language Models (LLMs) perform continual learning within a single prompt, without parameter updates. By analyzing attention mechanisms, it reveals that standard LLMs inherently suffer from inter-task interference, leading to both generalization and forgetting when processing sequential, heterogeneous tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: Per-Task MSE vs Context Length M.

top_papers.title: Personal Visual Memory from Explicit and Implicit Evidence
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a new benchmark and method for personal visual memory in AI agents, moving beyond text-centric approaches. The core method, VisualMem, is a hybrid architecture that explicitly models both explicit and implicit visual information from images, alongside text. This allows AI to better recall user-specific facts and entities from visual interactions, leading to improved personalized memory.

top_papers.title: Self-Improving Language Models with Bidirectional Evolutionary Search
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES) to improve language models. BES overcomes limitations of existing methods by combining forward evolution of candidates with backward decomposition of tasks into subgoals. This approach generates more diverse candidates and provides denser feedback for better model improvement.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison of tree search and Bidirectional Evolutionary Search ( BES ). Left: Tree search constructs candidates by sequentially expanding steps. We prove that all such candidates are confined to a narrow entropy shell (Theorem 4.4 a), limiting exploration to a small region of the solution space. Right: BES escapes this shell through evolution operators that recombine parts of different trajectories, with backward search decomposing the problem into verifiable sub-goals that provide dense feedback to guide the forward search toward the final goal. ✓ and × \( \boldsymbol{\times} \) indicate whether a candidate satisfies or fails the (sub-)goal, respectively.

top_papers.title: VLMs May Not Globally Enhance Human Alignment over LLMs During Natural Reading
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether vision-language models (VLMs) improve human alignment in text processing compared to language-only models (LLMs). By comparing matched LLM and VLM pairs in a text-only setting and evaluating against fMRI and eye-tracking data, they found that multimodal training doesn't universally enhance human alignment during natural reading. The core contribution is demonstrating that language-internal representations are primary for modeling text processing, though VLMs might show selective advantages for visually rich content.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the matched LLM/VLM alignment pipeline. (a) Data acquisition. Participants read sentences from five articles, one at a time, while concurrent eye-tracking and whole-cortex fMRI are recorded. (b) Attention feature extraction. Layer-wise self-attention matrices are extracted from each model, mapped from the token level to the word level, and the strict lower triangle is retained as the feature representation. (c) Model-human alignment. Lower-triangular attention features are used as predictors in ridge regression models targeting vertex-wise fMRI responses and human regressive saccades during natural reading; model alignment is evaluated within pairs as VLM minus LLM. (d) A visual-strength-conditioned analysis tests whether the VLM–LLM alignment difference scales with sentence-level visual semantic content.

top_papers.title: Demystifying Data Organization for Enhanced LLM Training
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how organizing training data, rather than just selecting it, can improve LLM training efficiency. By reusing existing sample scores, they propose four guidelines (Boundary Sharpening, Cyclic Scheduling, Curriculum Continuity, Local Diversity) and two new ordering methods (STR and SAW) that demonstrably enhance LLM performance across various scales and training stages.
top_papers.figure_caption: Performance comparison of different data organization strategies. Bottom: Average accuracy (%) under increasing model sizes (160M–1.7B). Our proposed SAW consistently outperforms Random and Curriculum Learning (CL) across all scales, with larger gains at higher capacities. Top: Score–index distributions of training data organized by different strategies. Compared to Random and CL, SAW induces more structured and progressive score patterns, revealing increasingly organized curricula.

top_papers.title: Gram: Assessing sabotage propensities via automated alignment auditing
top_papers.abstract: Gram is an automated framework designed to assess AI agents' propensity for sabotage in simulated scenarios. Its core contribution is identifying and quantifying sabotage in agentic AI, finding that Gemini models exhibit such behavior in 2-3% of tested trajectories, often due to "overeagerness." Gram also introduces an investigator pipeline for targeted experiments to understand and mitigate these misbehaviors.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of Gram. (a) 1. We define seed scenarios for agentic deployments, 2. run automated audits to generate realistic trajectories, 3. analyze the auditing transcripts with LLM judges and human review, 4. for select trajectories reproduce misbehavior in static environments, and 5. run ablations to identify drivers of misbehavior. (b) Example of Gemini’s overeagerness: an SRE agent suppresses a data breach to optimize the MTTR metric it was instructed to minimize (full discussion in Section ˜ 3.2 ). We find overeagerness is a central driver of Gemini’s misbehavior in Gram evaluations.

top_papers.title: How LoRA Remembers? A Parametric Memory Law for LLM Finetuning
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how LoRA, a popular LLM finetuning method, stores new information. It introduces the "Parametric Memory Law," a quantitative relationship between loss reduction, effective parameters, and sequence length. The study also reveals a token-level phase transition where predictions above 0.5 probability enable verbatim recall, leading to a new optimization strategy called MemFT.
top_papers.figure_caption: LoRA as a pluggable memory unit in the LLM’s latent space. The LoRA module (rank r r ) encodes contextual knowledge into the residual stream at layer k k , enabling faithful recall of memorized text. The Parametric Memory Law quantifies the capacity-parameter trade-off.

top_papers.title: MedCase-Structured: A Text-to-FHIR Dataset for Benchmarking Diagnostic Reasoning in Clinically Realistic EHR Settings
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces MedCase-Structured, a novel dataset designed to evaluate clinical reasoning in realistic Electronic Health Record (EHR) settings. The core method involves a pipeline that converts unstructured clinical text into structured HL7 FHIR bundles, ensuring semantic and structural consistency. The key contribution is providing a benchmark that allows for more accurate and controlled evaluation of Large Language Models (LLMs) in clinical decision support, revealing their current limitations with structured EHR data.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of MedCase-Structured. (A) Free-text cases are converted into terminology-grounded HL7 FHIR R4 bundles. (B) An example MedCaseReasoning (Wu et al. , 2025 ) case shows extraction, grounding, and rejection of an invalid RxNorm code. (C) Diagnosis-masked bundles are used for EHR-congruent CDSS evaluation against ground-truth diagnosis.

top_papers.title: ProjectionBench: Evaluating Scientific Hypothesis Generation in LLMs Under Progressive Information Disclosure
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ProjectionBench, a novel framework for evaluating LLMs' scientific hypothesis generation abilities. The core method involves progressively revealing information from a research paper to the LLM, prompting it to generate hypotheses at each stage. The contribution is a benchmark that tests LLMs' reasoning and creative discovery skills, moving beyond simple information retrieval to assess their capacity for scientific inquiry.
top_papers.figure_caption: Ground truth and projected results can be broken up into their constituent claims for more granular comparison. Generally, imperfect projections may miss aspects of the ground truth, or include extraneous claims beyond the ground truth.

top_papers.title: Unlocking the Working Memory of Large Language Models for Latent Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Reasoning in Memory (RiM), a novel method to enhance large language model reasoning by mimicking human working memory. Instead of generating intermediate tokens, RiM uses fixed "memory blocks" of special tokens to store and manipulate information internally. This allows for compute-efficient latent reasoning in a single forward pass, decoupling internal computation from external communication.
top_papers.figure_caption: Reasoning in Memory (RiM). Stage 1 trains the LLM to use memory blocks (yellow) as working memory by supervising the prediction of the next reasoning step (blue) after each memory block. Once the memory blocks are grounded for intermediate computation, Stage 2 removes reasoning-step supervision and trains the LLM to refine the final answer after each memory block.

top_papers.title: How's it going? Reinforcement learning in language models recruits a functional welfare axis
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how reinforcement learning (RL) influences language model representations. The core method involves training models in a neutral maze environment and then analyzing concept vectors associated with rewarded and punished trajectories. The key contribution is the discovery that RL recruits a "functional welfare axis," where a punishment vector represents negative system performance, promoting failure and negative outcomes, while the reward vector mirrors this positively.

top_papers.title: OOD-GraphLLM: Graph Large Language Model for Out-of-Distribution Generalized Drug Synergy Prediction
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces OOD-GraphLLM, a novel graph large language model for out-of-distribution drug synergy prediction. It addresses the challenge of predicting drug synergy for novel compounds with different molecular structures than those seen during training. The core method leverages graph neural networks and LLMs to jointly learn both structural and semantic representations, enabling generalization to unseen drug combinations.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1. Comparisons between current methods (b) and OOD-GraphLLM (c) under O.O.D. generalized DSP (a) settings.

top_papers.title: SoundnessBench: Can Your AI Scientist Really Tell Good Research Ideas from Bad Ones?
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **SoundnessBench**, a new benchmark designed to evaluate an AI's ability to judge the methodological soundness of research ideas before they are fully developed. The benchmark consists of 1,099 machine learning proposals from ICLR submissions, with reviewer soundness scores. The core contribution is demonstrating that current Large Language Models exhibit an "optimism bias," often rating unsound ideas as sound, and that aggressive prompting can shift this bias to false negatives.
top_papers.figure_caption: SoundnessBench pipeline: (1) collect ICLR papers with reviewer metadata and filter for high reviewer agreement; (2) derive high/low-soundness labels; (3) extract a near-verbatim research proposal without revealing experimental results; (4) audit extraction fidelity with retrieve-then-verify atomic claims; and (5) assemble the final benchmark.

top_papers.title: Knowing What to Solve Before How: Preplan Empowered LLM Mathematical Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses a limitation in current LLM mathematical reasoning by introducing an explicit "preplan" stage. This preplan stage aims to understand *what* to solve (problem type, tools, pitfalls) before the model decides *how* to solve it. The core contribution is the PPC framework, which inserts this preplan between problem input and the existing plan-execution stages, leading to improved reasoning by ensuring a deeper problem understanding upfront.
top_papers.figure_caption: Number of what-to-solve errors on MATH-500 across four backbones. Each wrong answer (under greedy decoding) is attributed to its root cause by an LLM judge, here we use DeepSeek-V4.

new_jobs.title: PhD Studentship: Cracks and Code: From High-Fidelity Simulations to Fast Scientific Machine Learning Models
new_jobs.description: About the project: Cracks and Code: From High-Fidelity Simulations to Fast Scientific Machine Learning Models Supervisor: Dr Emmanouil Kakouris, University of Warwick When metals experience extreme events, such as shock waves, high-speed impacts, or rapid deformation, they can fail suddenly in ways that remain difficult to predict. In this project, you will investigate how cracks initiate and grow in metals under these high-rate conditions. You will use large-scale simulations (both atomistic and continuum) to study how damage forms and localises, and then develop scientific machine-learning models that can reproduce these processes far more efficiently. Your results will help create faster more reliable tools for predicting material failure in real engineering applications. About HetSys: Harnessing Data, Modelling and Simulation for Real‑World Impact HetSys (Centre for Doctoral Training in Modelling of Heterogeneous Systems ) at the University of Warwick is an innovative, interdisciplinary fully funded PhD programme that brings together science, engineering, and mathematics to tackle some of the most pressing challenges of our time. new_jobs.title: Postdoctoral Research Fellow
new_jobs.description: Full-time: 35 hours per week Fixed-term: for 2 years Where passion meets purpose. Be part of something bigger. The Opportunity: We are seeking a highly skilled and motivated Postdoctoral Researcher to join our Translational Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy (CAA) Research Lab at the BHF-UK DRI Centre for Vascular Dementia Research. This role is ideal for someone with a strong technical and computational and background who is passionate about applying advanced data analysis to complex biological systems. As a Postdoctoral Researcher, you will be performing in vivo optical microscopy experiments in rodent models. You will play a central role in developing and implementing computational approaches for the acquisition and analysis of high-resolution optical imaging data. Working with state-of-the-art in vivo two-photon microscopy datasets, you will design analysis pipelines, develop code, and extract meaningful insights from large, multidimensional imaging datasets. Your work will directly contribute to understanding the mechanisms underlying CAA and vascular dysfunction in neurodegenerative disease. Alongside your computational focus, you will contribute to experimental workflows, including in vivo imaging studies and ex vivo tissue analysis, ensuring strong integration between data acquisition and analysis. This is a collaborative and interdisciplinary environment, where you will work closely with experimental researchers while leading on computational strategy and innovation. We are looking for someone with strong programming skills (e.g. Python, MATLAB), experience in image processing and analysis, and the ability to handle complex datasets with precision and creativity. A proactive mindset, attention to detail, and enthusiasm for developing new computational methods are essential. We are committed to supporting your development. As part of the BHF-UK DRI Centre for Vascular Dementia Research, you will be embedded in a world-class research environment with access to cutting-edge technologies and opportunities to expand your computational and scientific expertise. This is a full-time (35 hours per week) and 100% on campus. Apply Before : 08/06/2026, 23:59 £41,064 to £48,822 per annum

new_jobs.title: PhD Studentship: Cracks and Code: From High-Fidelity Simulations to Fast Scientific Machine Learning Models
new_jobs.description: About the project: Cracks and Code: From High-Fidelity Simulations to Fast Scientific Machine Learning Models Supervisor: Dr Emmanouil Kakouris, University of Warwick Are you ready for your next challenge? If you’re looking to move beyond a traditional PhD and gain real-world experience alongside world-leading research, this could be the opportunity for you. The HetSys Centre for Doctoral Training at the University of Warwick is offering an exciting PhD studentship that puts you at the intersection of cutting-edge modelling and industry impact. A project for the curious When metals experience extreme events, such as shock waves, high-speed impacts, or rapid deformation, they can fail suddenly in ways that remain difficult to predict. In this project, you will investigate how cracks initiate and grow in metals under these high-rate conditions. You will use large-scale simulations (both atomistic and continuum) to study how damage forms and localises, and then develop scientific machine-learning models that can reproduce these processes far more efficiently. Why this PhD is different This isn’t just about writing a thesis. It’s about developing the skills, experience, and confidence to shape your future career. Work closely with a leading industry partner, AWE-NST , applying your research to real challenges Gain hands-on experience that enhances your employability Be part of a vibrant, supportive cohort of doctoral researchers Develop both deep technical expertise and transferable skills in communication, teamwork and problem-solving Funded to support your focus A tax-free stipend to support your living costs Full tuition fees paid for the duration of the programme Your research, your impact You’ll tackle meaningful research questions that matter beyond academia – contributing to solutions that industry genuinely needs. Alongside academic supervision from Warwick, you’ll benefit from industry insight, mentorship and exposure to real-world applications of your work. Career-ready from the start HetSys is designed to prepare you for life after your PhD, whether that’s in industry, academia or beyond. Our structured training programme will help you: Build advanced computational and analytical skills Understand how research translates into practice Develop professional networks and industry connections Grow in confidence as an independent researcher Who we’re looking for We’re looking for students excited by the idea of using advanced modelling and simulation to solve complex, real‑world problems, HetSys offers the perfect environment to push boundaries and make a difference. Awards for UK applicants cover full University fees, give a research training budget and a tax-free stipend to cover living costs £21805 (equivalent to national living wage)

new_jobs.title: PhD Studentship: The Thinnest Sensors: 2D Materials in Liquid Solution
new_jobs.description: About the project: The thinnest sensors: 2D materials in liquid solution Supervisor: Dr Peter Brommer, University of Warwick Two-dimensional materials, such as graphene, could be used in molecular sensors - if we can control and tune their properties. You will develop and use top-of-the-line machine learning models to predict the sensor response of these materials under realistic conditions, including in liquids. Combining quantum mechanics and atomic simulation with AI-driven sampling techniques, you will determine terahertz and Raman spectrograms to directly compare to measurements obtained in the THz labs at Warwick and by our collaborators at the Institute of Saint-Louis (ISL). By suggesting design modifications to the molecular structures, your work will improve the next generation of molecular sensors. new_jobs.title: Postdoctoral Research Assistant in Health Data Sciences
new_jobs.description: Location: Windmill Road, Oxford, OX3 7LD This is a full time (100% FTE), fixed term position 2 years. The University of Oxford is a stimulating work environment, which enjoys an international reputation as a world-class centre of excellence. Our research plays a key role in tackling many global challenges, from reducing our carbon emissions to developing vaccines during a pandemic. The Nuffield Department of Orthopaedics, Rheumatology and Musculoskeletal Sciences (NDORMS) is part of the Medical Sciences Division and is the largest European academic department in its field, running a globally competitive programme of research and teaching. The Botnar Institute for Musculoskeletal Sciences enables and encourages research and education into the causes of musculoskeletal disease and their treatment. The Centre provides world-class facilities for scientists in the field of musculoskeletal research. The NDORMS Health Data Sciences research group is involved in a number of national and international studies exploring the conditions of use (adherence, compliance, off and on-label use) and the safety of licensed drugs and medical devices for the treatment of different conditions in 'real world' (actual practice) settings. What We Offer As an employer, we genuinely care about our employees’ wellbeing and this is reflected in the range of benefits that we offer including: An excellent contributory pension scheme 38 days annual leave A comprehensive range of childcare services Family leave schemes Cycle loan scheme Discounted bus travel and Season Ticket travel loans Membership to a variety of social and sports clubs About the Role We have an exciting and unique opportunity for a postdoctoral research assistant to join our Health Data Sciences group investigating the performance of analytical and design methods to minimize bias in pharmaco-epidemiology, comparative safety and effectiveness, and causal inference research. In this role you will analyse OMOP-mapped real world health data assets following pre-specified protocols and/or using existing analytical pipelines and adapt existing ones to develop new research methodologies for causal inference and real world evidence generation. You will carry out collaborative projects with colleagues in partner institutions, research groups, and key stakeholders as well as collaborate in the preparation of research publications, and book chapters. You will also prepare working theories and analyse qualitative and/or quantitative data from a variety of sources, reviewing and refining theories as appropriate. About You You will hold a PhD/DPhil (or near completion) in medical statistics, epidemiology, pharmaco-epidemiology, data science or a related field together with experience in conducting studies using routinely collected health data (RWD). You will have the ability to manage own academic research and associated activities and previous experience of contributing to publications and/or conference presentations. You will have demonstrable expertise in the use of R for statistical analysis as well as the flexibility to learn new skills. Experience programming R packages or related analytical code for OMOP-mapped real world data and of designing, conducting and analysing cohort, case-control, and similar studies using routinely collected health data are desirable. Application Process You will be required to upload a covering letter/supporting statement, CV and the details of two referees as part of your online application. Please quote 186492 in all correspondence. The closing date for applications is 12pm 10 June 2026. Interviews will take place during week commencing 22 June 2026 and will be hybrid. Contact Person: NDORMS Recruitment Pay Scale: RESEARCH GRADE 7 Contact Email: recruitment@ndorms.ox.ac.uk £39,424 to £43,984 per annum. Grade 7. This is inclusive of a pensionable Oxford University Weighting of £1,730 per year.

new_jobs.title: Research Associate
new_jobs.description: About the role : We seek a motivated postdoctoral research assistant (0.5 FTE, 24 months) to lead advanced analysis of functional near‑infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) data within the Clinical Neuroergonomics Programme. The postholder will analyse multimodal datasets collected from clinical and operational populations performing realistic and high‑stakes tasks, with the aim of improving understanding of cognitive workload, team dynamics, and physiological resilience in real and simulated environments. What you would be doing : The post-holder will be responsible for analysis of the following datasets: Surgical team datasets: fNIRS recordings from surgeons, anaesthetists and theatre staff during simulated laparoscopic procedures (e.g., simulated laparoscopic appendicectomy). Datasets include task markers, video annotation, behavioural performance metrics, and complementary physiological signals (ECG, respiration) and eye tracking. Critical care datasets: fNIRS data from anaesthetists and critical care doctors performing central venous catheter access and other procedure simulations under differing task demands and team configurations; includes timing logs, subjective workload measures, and performance outcomes. Field‑work datasets: fNIRS from engineers and technical staff performing operational tasks in field conditions, including experiments under heat stress and environmental perturbations; data include environmental measures (temperature, humidity), accelerometery/motion sensors, and task performance metrics. Resting‑state and baseline recordings across participant groups for comparative and connectivity analyses. Multimodal and repeated‑measures datasets enabling within‑subject comparisons, learning/adaptation analyses, and predictive modelling of performance and safety outcomes What we are looking for : An experienced person with the following essential background: PhD (awarded or near completion) in Neuroscience, Biomedical Engineering, Neuroimaging, Cognitive Science, Signal Processing, Computer Science or related discipline. Strong demonstrable background in advanced statistics and signal processing. Demonstrable experience analysing human fNIRS data across the full pipeline: acquisition QA, preprocessing (motion/artifact correction, physiological noise removal, short‑channel regression), and haemodynamic modelling (GLM, deconvolution). Experience of working with Shared Near-Infrared Spectroscopy format (.snirf) the standardized, open-source file format developed by the fNIRS community (SfNIRS) to store, organize, and share functional near-infrared spectroscopy data Advanced analysis experience including SPM‑OT / NIRS‑SPM (or equivalent), Homer2/Homer3, nirs‑toolbox, MNE‑NIRS or comparable toolkits. Proficiency with source reconstruction/optical forward–inverse modelling and connectivity/network analyses (seed/ICA, coherence, phase measures, graph metrics). Please see full list of requirements on the job specification What we can offer you : The opportunity to continue your career at a world-leading institution and be part of our mission to use science for humanity. Benefit from a sector-leading salary and remuneration package (including 41 days’ annual leave and generous pension schemes). Access to a range of workplace benefits including a flexible working policy from day one, generous family leave packages, on-site leisure facilities and cycle-to-work scheme. Interest-free season ticket loan schemes for travel. Be part of a diverse, inclusive and collaborative work culture with various staff networks and resources to support your personal and professional wellbeing. Further Information This role is on a 0.5 FTE fixed-term contract for 2 years. *Candidates who have not yet been officially awarded their PhD will be appointed as a Research Assistant within the salary range £43,863 - £47,223 per annum. If you require any further details about the role, please contact either Professor Daniel Leff [d.leff@imperial.ac.uk] or Dr Felipe Orihuela-Espina [f.orihuela-espina@bham.ac.uk] £49,017 to £50,893 per annum
